skills.title: Deploy an ERC-721 NFT Using Hardhat
skills.description: Scaffolds a Hardhat project manually, installs OpenZeppelin contracts and dotenv, creates a MyNFT.sol ERC-721 contract and Ignition deployment module, compiles with Cancun EVM version, and deploys to Polkadot Hub TestNet via Hardhat Ignition. Use when deploying an ERC-721 NFT to Polkadot Hub. Requires testnet PAS tokens and a funded EVM wallet. Trigger phrases: 'deploy NFT Polkadot', 'ERC-721 Hardhat', 'deploy non-fungible token', 'mint NFT Polkadot Hub'. Uses OpenZeppelin Contracts v5.4.0+ requiring evmVersion cancun. Do NOT use for ERC-20 tokens; use deploy-erc20-token-hardhat instead.

network: Polkadot Hub TestNet RPC: https://services.polkadothub-rpc.com/testnet (chain ID 420420417)
tokens: Testnet PAS tokens for gas fees — get from https://faucet.polkadot.io/ (TestNet base fee: 1000 gwei)
wallet: An EVM private key (0x-prefixed) for a funded testnet account

env_vars.name: PRIVATE_KEY
env_vars.description: 0x-prefixed EVM private key for the deployer account. Must be funded with testnet PAS. Never commit to version control.

steps.action: Create the project directory and initialize npm
steps.description: Create a new directory and initialize a Node.js project. Do not use 'npx hardhat init' — it is interactive and non-deterministic. Manual scaffolding is used instead.
Install Hardhat v2 toolchain, OpenZeppelin contracts (v5.4.0+), and dotenv for secure private key handling.

Create a .env file with an empty PRIVATE_KEY placeholder and add .env to .gitignore. Stop here and ask the user to edit .env directly — fill in PRIVATE_KEY with their 0x-prefixed EVM private key for an account funded with testnet PAS. Do NOT ask for the private key in chat. Wait for the user to confirm .env is filled before proceeding.
Create the contracts and ignition/modules directories that Hardhat expects.
Compile MyNFT.sol. If compilation fails with 'Invalid opcode: MCOPY', ensure evmVersion: 'cancun' is set in hardhat.config.ts and recompile.

steps.action: Deploy the NFT contract to Polkadot Hub TestNet
commands: npx hardhat ignition deploy ignition/modules/MyNFT.ts --network polkadotTestnet
steps.description: Deploy the ERC-721 contract via Hardhat Ignition. Ignition will prompt to confirm the target network and chain ID — delegate this prompt to the user. Save the contract address printed on success.

error_patterns.pattern: CompilationError: Invalid opcode: MCOPY
error_patterns.cause: OpenZeppelin Contracts v5.4.0+ uses the mcopy opcode requiring Cancun EVM version.
error_patterns.resolution: In hardhat.config.ts set evmVersion: 'cancun' inside solidity.settings, then run 'npx hardhat compile' again.

error_patterns.pattern: Error: insufficient funds / Priority is too low
error_patterns.cause: Deployer account has insufficient PAS, or gasPrice is below the TestNet 1000 gwei base fee.
error_patterns.resolution: Get testnet tokens at https://faucet.polkadot.io/. Verify hardhat.config.ts polkadotTestnet block includes gasPrice: 5000000000000.

error_patterns.pattern: IGN401: Transaction dropped / Transaction Already Imported
error_patterns.cause: Ignition lost track of deployment due to missing gasPrice or requiredConfirmations: 0.
error_patterns.resolution: Delete ignition/deployments/, ensure gasPrice: 5000000000000 and ignition.requiredConfirmations: 1 in config, then redeploy.

error_patterns.pattern: Error: vars is not defined / Cannot read properties of undefined
error_patterns.cause: hardhat.config.ts still uses Hardhat's vars.get() system rather than dotenv.
error_patterns.resolution: Add 'import "dotenv/config";' as the first line of hardhat.config.ts and replace vars.get('PRIVATE_KEY') with process.env.PRIVATE_KEY as string.

skills.title: Deploy an ERC-721 NFT Using Remix IDE
skills.description: Guides the user through deploying an ERC-721 NFT contract via Remix IDE (browser-based) connected to MetaMask on Polkadot Hub TestNet. Use when preferring no local toolchain or rapid prototyping. Covers contract creation using OpenZeppelin ERC-721, compilation in Remix, and deployment via MetaMask injected provider. Trigger phrases: 'deploy NFT Remix', 'ERC-721 Remix Polkadot', 'mint NFT using browser', 'Remix NFT Polkadot Hub'. All steps are GUI-based — agent delegates browser actions to the user. Do NOT use for automated or CLI-based deployments; use deploy-erc721-nft-hardhat instead.

network: Polkadot Hub TestNet (chain ID 420420417)
tokens: Testnet PAS tokens for gas — get from https://faucet.polkadot.io/
wallet: MetaMask browser extension installed and configured for Polkadot Hub TestNet
Testnet account funded with PAS tokens

steps.action: Open Remix IDE and create the contract file

steps.description: Delegate to the user: (1) Click the 'Solidity Compiler' icon in the left panel. (2) Click the 'Compile MyNFT.sol' button. Success: the compiler icon shows a green checkmark. If errors appear, check that the contract code was pasted correctly and the Solidity version is 0.8.22+.

steps.action: Connect MetaMask to Polkadot Hub TestNet
steps.description: Delegate to the user: (1) Click 'Deploy & Run Transactions' in the left panel. (2) In the 'Environment' dropdown, select 'Injected Provider - MetaMask'. (3) Approve the MetaMask connection popup. (4) Confirm MetaMask is on Polkadot Hub TestNet (chain ID 420420417). If not, add it via MetaMask Settings > Networks with RPC https://services.polkadothub-rpc.com/testnet. Confirm the funded account is visible.

Delegate to the user: (1) Ensure 'MyNFT' is selected in the Contract dropdown. (2) Expand the Deploy section and enter the constructor argument: the owner address (typically your MetaMask wallet address). (3) Click 'Deploy'. (4) Confirm the transaction in MetaMask. Once deployed, the contract appears in 'Deployed Contracts'. Record the contract address.

error_patterns.pattern: MetaMask: Wrong network / chain ID mismatch
error_patterns.cause: MetaMask is connected to a different network than Polkadot Hub TestNet.
error_patterns.resolution: Switch to or add Polkadot Hub TestNet in MetaMask with chain ID 420420417 and RPC https://services.polkadothub-rpc.com/testnet.

error_patterns.pattern: insufficient funds for gas
error_patterns.cause: The MetaMask account does not have enough PAS tokens to pay for deployment gas.
error_patterns.resolution: Get testnet PAS tokens from https://faucet.polkadot.io/ for the connected account, then retry.

error_patterns.pattern: Compilation errors in Remix
error_patterns.cause: Contract code syntax error or Solidity version mismatch.
error_patterns.resolution: Verify the contract code was pasted correctly. In the Solidity Compiler tab, ensure the compiler version is 0.8.22 or later.

skills.title: Use Foundry with Polkadot Hub
skills.description: Installs the Foundry nightly build (required for native Polkadot chain support), initializes a Forge project, configures foundry.toml for Polkadot Hub TestNet, deploys a contract via forge create, verifies it on Blockscout or Routescan, and interacts with it using cast. Use when building or deploying Solidity contracts on Polkadot Hub with the Foundry toolkit. Trigger phrases: 'Foundry Polkadot', 'forge deploy', 'forge create Polkadot', 'cast call Polkadot Hub'. Requires Foundry nightly — stable release lacks Polkadot chain definitions.

runtime: Unix-based OS (Linux or macOS) or Windows WSL
network: Polkadot Hub TestNet (chain ID 420420417) — supported natively by Foundry nightly as --chain polkadot-testnet
tokens: Testnet PAS tokens for deployment — get from https://faucet.polkadot.io/ (TestNet base fee: 1000 gwei)
wallet: An EVM private key (0x-prefixed) for a funded testnet account

env_vars.name: PRIVATE_KEY
env_vars.description: 0x-prefixed EVM private key for the deployer account. Loaded via 'source .env' into the shell session. Must be funded with testnet PAS. Never commit to version control.

steps.description: Install foundryup then the Foundry nightly build. Nightly is required — stable does not include native Polkadot chain support. If 'foundryup' is not found after the first command, run 'source ~/.bashrc' (or ~/.zshrc) or start a new terminal. Verify with 'forge --version'.
Create a new Foundry project with src/ (contracts), script/ (deployment scripts), test/ (Solidity tests), lib/ (dependencies), and foundry.toml. A sample Counter.sol is placed in src/.

steps.action: Configure foundry.toml for Polkadot Hub TestNet
steps.description:
Replace the contents of 'foundry.toml' with the following. Choose Blockscout (no API key) or Routescan:

Blockscout:
```toml
[profile.default]
src = "src"
out = "out"
libs = ["lib"]
solc_version = "0.8.28"

[etherscan]
polkadot-testnet = { key = "", url = "https://blockscout-testnet.polkadot.io/api?" }
```

Routescan:
```toml
[profile.default]
src = "src"
out = "out"
libs = ["lib"]
solc_version = "0.8.28"

[etherscan]
polkadot-testnet = { key = "verifyContract", url = "https://api.routescan.io/v2/network/testnet/evm/420420417/etherscan" }
```

With this config, use --chain polkadot-testnet in commands without specifying the RPC URL explicitly.

steps.action: Create .env file and load private key
steps.description: Create a .env file with an empty PRIVATE_KEY placeholder and add .env to .gitignore. Stop here and ask the user to edit .env with their 0x-prefixed private key for a testnet-funded account. Do NOT ask for the private key in chat. After confirmation, instruct the user to run 'source .env' in their terminal to load the variable into their shell session.
Compile all Solidity contracts in src/. Artifacts output to out/. The sample Counter.sol should compile without errors.

steps.action: Deploy a contract to Polkadot Hub TestNet
steps.description: Deploy the Counter contract to Polkadot Hub TestNet. Replace 'src/Counter.sol:Counter' with your contract path and name if deploying a custom contract. Save the 'Deployed to: 0x...' address — needed for verification and interaction.

steps.action: Verify the contract on the block explorer
steps.description:
Replace INSERT_CONTRACT_ADDRESS with the address from step 6.

Blockscout:
```bash
forge verify-contract INSERT_CONTRACT_ADDRESS src/Counter.sol:Counter --chain polkadot-testnet
```

Routescan:
```bash
forge verify-contract INSERT_CONTRACT_ADDRESS src/Counter.sol:Counter --verifier-url 'https://api.routescan.io/v2/network/testnet/evm/420420417/etherscan' --etherscan-api-key 'verifyContract' --chain polkadot-testnet
```

Expected output: 'Response: OK' and a URL to the verified contract.

steps.action: Interact with the contract using Cast
steps.description:
Replace INSERT_CONTRACT_ADDRESS and INSERT_ACCOUNT_ADDRESS with actual values.

Read from contract:
```bash
cast call INSERT_CONTRACT_ADDRESS 'number()(uint256)' --chain polkadot-testnet
```

Write to contract:
```bash
cast send INSERT_CONTRACT_ADDRESS 'setNumber(uint256)' 42 --chain polkadot-testnet --private-key $PRIVATE_KEY
```

Check balance:
```bash
cast balance INSERT_ACCOUNT_ADDRESS --chain polkadot-testnet
```

error_patterns.pattern: foundryup: command not found / forge: command not found
error_patterns.cause: Foundry was installed but PATH was not updated for the current shell session.
error_patterns.resolution: Run 'source ~/.bashrc' (or '~/.zshrc' on macOS) or start a new terminal.

error_patterns.pattern: Error: Chain 'polkadot-testnet' not found / unknown chain
error_patterns.cause: Using the Foundry stable release instead of nightly. Stable does not include Polkadot chain definitions.
error_patterns.resolution: Install the nightly build: run 'foundryup --version nightly'.

error_patterns.pattern: Error: Priority is too low / insufficient funds
error_patterns.cause: Account has insufficient PAS or gas price is below the TestNet 1000 gwei base fee.
error_patterns.resolution: Get testnet PAS from https://faucet.polkadot.io/. The Foundry nightly build handles gas pricing automatically for Polkadot chains.

error_patterns.pattern: forge test: tests pass locally but fail on TestNet
error_patterns.cause: forge test runs against Anvil (Ethereum semantics), not Polkadot Hub. Existential deposit and gas model differ.
error_patterns.resolution: Use forge test only for unit testing contract logic. For Polkadot-specific behavior, test against a local dev node or TestNet.

skills.title: Spawn and Test a Parachain Network with Zombienet
skills.description: Installs Zombienet (executable, Nix, or Docker), downloads polkadot and polkadot-parachain binaries, writes a TOML network config defining relay chain validators and parachain collators, spawns the network via the native provider, writes .zndsl test files using Zombienet's DSL, and runs the test suite. Use when spawning and testing ephemeral Polkadot SDK networks locally. Trigger phrases: 'Zombienet', 'spawn local parachain', 'test parachain network', 'local relay chain Zombienet', 'zndsl test'. Requires polkadot and polkadot-parachain binaries.

runtime: Unix-based OS (Linux or macOS) or Windows WSL
polkadot and polkadot-parachain binaries — downloaded via: zombienet setup polkadot polkadot-parachain

steps.description: Download the Zombienet executable for Linux x64. For macOS replace 'zombienet-linux-x64' with 'zombienet-macos'. For macOS Gatekeeper issues run: xattr -d com.apple.quarantine zombienet. The 'zombienet version' command confirms successful installation. Docker alternative: docker run -it --rm -v $(pwd):/home/nonroot/zombie-net/host-current-files paritytech/zombienet

Download the polkadot and polkadot-parachain binaries for the native provider. Zombienet downloads these to a local path and adds them to PATH for the session. Verify availability: 'polkadot --version' and 'polkadot-parachain --version'.

Create 'network.toml' with a basic network configuration. This defines a relay chain with two validators and a parachain with one collator. If using the Polkadot Hub TestNet chain spec first download it:
```bash
wget https://paseo-r2.zondax.ch/chain-specs/paseo-asset-hub.json
```

Then create network.toml:
```toml
[settings]
timeout = 1000

[relaychain]
chain = "paseo"
default_command = "polkadot"

  [[relaychain.nodes]]
  name = "alice"
  validator = true

  [[relaychain.nodes]]
  name = "bob"
  validator = true

[[parachains]]
id = 1000
chain_spec_path = "./paseo-asset-hub.json"

  [parachains.collator]
  name = "collator-01"
  command = "polkadot-parachain"
```

Adjust id, chain_spec_path, and command fields to match your target network. For relay-only tests, omit the [[parachains]] section.

Spawn the local network using the native provider. Zombienet will: (1) locate or download binaries, (2) generate chain specifications, (3) start relay chain validators, (4) register and start parachain collators, (5) display RPC endpoints. The first relay chain node is typically accessible at ws://127.0.0.1:9944. Run this in a separate terminal — the network persists until you stop it with Ctrl+C.

Create 'network-test.zndsl' to test the spawned network:

```
Description: Basic network health test
Network: ./network.toml
Creds: config

alice: is up
bob: is up
alice: parachain 1000 is registered within 200 seconds
alice: parachain 1000 block height is at least 10 within 300 seconds
```

Common assertion types: node health ('alice: is up'), parachain registration, block height, metrics ('alice: reports node_roles is 4'), and log patterns ('alice: log line matches glob "Imported #1" within 10 seconds'). Remove parachain assertions if your network has no parachain.

Execute the test suite against the running network. Each assertion is evaluated in order; Zombienet reports PASS or FAIL. Exit code 0 = all pass, 1 = any failure. Ensure the network from step 4 is still running before executing tests.

error_patterns.pattern: polkadot: binary not found / command not found
error_patterns.cause: polkadot or polkadot-parachain binaries are not in PATH.
error_patterns.resolution: Run 'zombienet setup polkadot polkadot-parachain' to download the binaries, then add them to PATH: export PATH=$PATH:<path-printed-by-setup>.

error_patterns.pattern: macOS: cannot be opened because it is from an unidentified developer
error_patterns.cause: macOS Gatekeeper is blocking the Zombienet executable.
error_patterns.resolution: Run: xattr -d com.apple.quarantine zombienet

error_patterns.pattern: Timeout: network did not start within timeout seconds
error_patterns.cause: Binary download was slow or the chain spec file is missing or invalid.
error_patterns.resolution: Increase the timeout value in the [settings] section of network.toml. Verify chain_spec_path exists and is valid.

error_patterns.pattern: Test FAIL: parachain X is not registered within N seconds
error_patterns.cause: Parachain onboarding takes time or the collator failed to start.
error_patterns.resolution: Increase the 'within N seconds' timeout in the .zndsl assertion. Review Zombienet logs for collator startup errors.

skills.title: Deploy and Interact with Smart Contracts Using Web3.js
skills.description: Sets up a Node.js project with Web3.js and solc, creates a Storage Solidity contract, configures a provider for Polkadot Hub TestNet, compiles to EVM bytecode, deploys, and interacts via read/write scripts. Use when integrating Polkadot Hub with Web3.js. Trigger phrases: 'Web3.js Polkadot', 'deploy contract Web3', 'web3js smart contract Polkadot'. NOTE: Web3.js has been sunset — for new projects use deploy-contracts-ethers-js or deploy-contracts-viem instead. All code files require substituting INSERT_* placeholders before execution.

network: Polkadot Hub TestNet RPC: https://services.polkadothub-rpc.com/testnet (chain ID 420420417)
tokens: Testnet PAS tokens for deployment gas — get from https://faucet.polkadot.io/ (TestNet base fee: 1000 gwei)
wallet: An EVM private key (0x-prefixed) for a funded testnet account

env_vars.name: PRIVATE_KEY
env_vars.description: 0x-prefixed EVM private key for the deployer account. Used in deploy.js and updateStorage.js. Must be funded with testnet PAS. Never commit to version control.

steps.action: Create project directory and initialize npm
steps.description: Create a new directory and initialize a Node.js project.
Install the Web3.js library for blockchain interaction and solc for compiling Solidity contracts to EVM bytecode.

steps.action: Create project directories and the Storage contract
Create the provider connection script and verify connectivity
steps.description:
Fetch the provider script: curl -sL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/polkadot-developers/polkadot-docs/main/code/smart-contracts/libraries/web3-js/connectToProvider.js -o scripts/connectToProvider.js

Open scripts/connectToProvider.js and make these substitutions:
(1) Replace INSERT_RPC_URL with 'https://services.polkadothub-rpc.com/testnet'
(2) Replace INSERT_CHAIN_ID with 420420417
(3) Replace INSERT_CHAIN_NAME with 'polkadot-hub-testnet'

Then verify: node scripts/connectToProvider.js
Expected output: 'Connected to chain ID: 420420417'

steps.action: Create the compile script and compile the contract
steps.description:
Fetch the compile script: curl -sL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/polkadot-developers/polkadot-docs/main/code/smart-contracts/libraries/web3-js/compile.js -o scripts/compile.js

The script reads contracts/Storage.sol, compiles it with solc, saves ABI to abis/Storage.json and bytecode to artifacts/Storage.bin. Then run it to compile.

steps.action: Create the deployment script and deploy the contract
steps.description:
Fetch the deploy script: curl -sL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/polkadot-developers/polkadot-docs/main/code/smart-contracts/libraries/web3-js/deploy.js -o scripts/deploy.js

Open deploy.js and substitute:
(1) Replace INSERT_PRIVATE_KEY with process.env.PRIVATE_KEY (do NOT hardcode the key)
(2) Ensure the RPC URL points to 'https://services.polkadothub-rpc.com/testnet'

Set PRIVATE_KEY in your environment: export PRIVATE_KEY=0x... (never commit this). Then deploy:
```bash
node scripts/deploy.js
```
Expected: contract address saved in contract-address.json. Record this address.

steps.action: Create the interaction script and interact with the contract
steps.description:
Fetch the interaction script: curl -sL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/polkadot-developers/polkadot-docs/main/code/smart-contracts/libraries/web3-js/updateStorage.js -o scripts/updateStorage.js

Open updateStorage.js and substitute:
(1) Replace INSERT_PRIVATE_KEY with process.env.PRIVATE_KEY
(2) Replace INSERT_CONTRACT_ADDRESS with the address from step 6, or ensure contract-address.json is read automatically
(3) Verify the ABI references 'abis/Storage.json'

Then run: node scripts/updateStorage.js
Expected: current stored value displayed, then updated to 1.

error_patterns.pattern: Error: insufficient funds / Priority is too low
error_patterns.cause: Deployer account has insufficient PAS or gas price is below the TestNet 1000 gwei base fee.
error_patterns.resolution: Get testnet PAS from https://faucet.polkadot.io/ and verify the funded account is used in deploy.js.

error_patterns.pattern: Error: Cannot find module 'web3'
error_patterns.cause: Web3.js dependency was not installed.
error_patterns.resolution: Run 'npm install web3' in the web3js-project directory.

error_patterns.pattern: Error reading ABI or bytecode file / file not found
error_patterns.cause: Compile step was skipped or output files are not in expected locations.
error_patterns.resolution: Run 'node scripts/compile.js' first to generate abis/Storage.json and artifacts/Storage.bin.

error_patterns.pattern: Error: cannot connect to provider / network timeout
error_patterns.cause: Wrong RPC URL or TestNet is temporarily unavailable.
error_patterns.resolution: Verify the RPC URL is 'https://services.polkadothub-rpc.com/testnet'. Check Polkadot status pages for TestNet outages.

supplementary_context.description: Load when the user wants a modern alternative to Web3.js or asks about contract interaction options.

pages.title: Deploy Contracts to Polkadot Hub with Ethers.js
pages.relevance: Recommended modern alternative to Web3.js for deploying and interacting with contracts on Polkadot Hub.

pages.title: viem for Polkadot Hub Smart Contracts
pages.relevance: TypeScript-native alternative library for Polkadot Hub contract interactions.

examples.scenario: Common scenario: deploy a Storage contract and interact with it
examples.user_says: Deploy a smart contract to Polkadot Hub using Web3.js
actions: Create web3js-project, npm init -y, install web3 and solc
Create contracts/Storage.sol with the storage contract
Create and run scripts/connectToProvider.js (replace INSERT_RPC_URL, INSERT_CHAIN_ID, INSERT_CHAIN_NAME)
Create and run scripts/compile.js to generate ABI and bytecode
Create scripts/deploy.js, replace INSERT_PRIVATE_KEY with process.env.PRIVATE_KEY, run it
Create scripts/updateStorage.js, replace INSERT_PRIVATE_KEY and INSERT_CONTRACT_ADDRESS, run it
examples.result: Storage contract deployed; read and write interactions confirmed

examples.scenario: Edge case: user wants a modern library instead of Web3.js
examples.user_says: Web3.js is sunset, what should I use instead?
actions: Note that Web3.js has been sunset
Recommend Ethers.js (deploy-contracts-ethers-js) or viem (deploy-contracts-viem) as actively maintained alternatives
examples.result: User directed to deploy-contracts-ethers-js or deploy-contracts-viem for modern contract interaction

skills.title: Bridge Assets from Ethereum to Polkadot via Snowbridge
skills.description: Bridges ERC-20 tokens from Ethereum mainnet to Polkadot Hub Asset Hub using the ParaSpell SDK's Snowbridge integration. Covers Node.js project setup, ERC-20 approval on Ethereum, building a Snowbridge transfer with the ParaSpell Builder API, signing with ethers.js, submitting, and monitoring bridge relay status. Use when a user needs to move WETH or other ERC-20s from Ethereum to the Polkadot ecosystem. Trigger phrases: 'bridge Ethereum to Polkadot', 'Snowbridge transfer', 'send WETH Polkadot Hub', 'ParaSpell Snowbridge', 'ERC-20 Polkadot bridge'. Requires real Ethereum mainnet ETH and ERC-20 tokens — no testnet path exists. Bridge relay takes 15-30 minutes. Uses dotenv for private key security.

network: Ethereum mainnet RPC endpoint (Infura, Alchemy, or public provider)
Polkadot Hub Asset Hub is the destination — no additional RPC needed on the Polkadot side
tokens: Real ERC-20 tokens (e.g., WETH) on Ethereum mainnet — testnet tokens cannot be used with Snowbridge
ETH on Ethereum mainnet to pay bridge transaction gas fees
wallet: Ethereum private key (0x-prefixed) for the sender account on Ethereum mainnet

env_vars.name: PRIVATE_KEY
env_vars.description: Ethereum private key (0x-prefixed) for the sender. Must hold ETH for gas and the ERC-20 tokens to bridge. Never commit to version control.

env_vars.name: ETH_RPC_URL
env_vars.description: Ethereum mainnet RPC URL (e.g., from Infura or Alchemy).

env_vars.name: DEST_POLKADOT_ADDRESS
env_vars.description: SS58-encoded destination address on Polkadot Hub Asset Hub.

commands: npm init -y && npm pkg set type=module
steps.description: Create 'snowbridge-transfer' and initialize it as an ESM Node.js project. ESM is required because @paraspell/sdk uses ES modules.
Install the ParaSpell SDK (includes Snowbridge API), Ethers.js v6 for Ethereum signing, and dotenv for secure key loading.

Create .env with three empty placeholders and .gitignore to exclude it. Stop and ask the user to edit .env directly: fill PRIVATE_KEY with an 0x-prefixed Ethereum private key that holds ETH and the token to bridge; ETH_RPC_URL with their Ethereum mainnet RPC endpoint; DEST_POLKADOT_ADDRESS with the SS58-formatted destination on Polkadot Hub Asset Hub. Do NOT ask the user to paste their private key in chat. Wait for confirmation before continuing.

steps.expected_output:
Sender: 0x...
ETH balance: ...
Submitting Snowbridge transfer...
Transaction hash: 0x...
Relay in progress...
steps.description: Run the script. If the Ethereum transaction fails with 'insufficient funds', add ETH to the sender wallet. After submission the Snowbridge relay picks up the lock event and mints tokens on Polkadot Hub Asset Hub — this takes 15-30 minutes. Do not re-run the script during this period to avoid double-submissions.

steps.action: Verify token arrival on Polkadot Hub
steps.description: After 15-30 minutes, verify arrival at DEST_POLKADOT_ADDRESS by connecting Polkadot.js Apps (https://polkadot.js.org/apps/?rpc=wss://polkadot-asset-hub-rpc.polkadot.io) to Asset Hub. Go to Developer > Chain State > foreignAssets > account and query using the WETH foreign asset multilocation. Alternatively search the destination address at https://assethub-polkadot.subscan.io/. If no tokens appear after 45 minutes, check bridge relay status at https://bridge.polkadot.io/ using the Ethereum transaction hash.

error_patterns.pattern: insufficient funds for intrinsic transaction cost
error_patterns.cause: Ethereum sender wallet does not have enough ETH to pay gas for the Snowbridge transaction.
error_patterns.resolution: Add ETH to the sender wallet on Ethereum mainnet. Gas costs for a Snowbridge transfer are typically 0.003-0.01 ETH at normal gas prices. Retry after funding.

error_patterns.pattern: buildEthTransfer is not a function / Builder().from is not a function
error_patterns.cause: The installed @paraspell/sdk version does not expose the Ethereum source chain builder or Snowbridge API.
error_patterns.resolution: Check your @paraspell/sdk version in package.json and consult https://paraspell.github.io/docs for the correct Snowbridge API. The Ethereum-to-Polkadot transfer API varies across SDK major versions.

error_patterns.pattern: Tokens not arrived on Polkadot Hub after 60 minutes
error_patterns.cause: Snowbridge relay is processing slowly or experiencing delays. Ethereum finality (15-20 minutes) plus relay processing adds latency.
error_patterns.resolution: Check https://bridge.polkadot.io/ with your Ethereum transaction hash. If status is 'Pending', wait up to 2 hours. If 'Failed', check the Polkadot Discord #snowbridge channel for relay outages. Do not resubmit without confirming tokens have not arrived — double-submission will lock funds.

error_patterns.pattern: ERC-20 token not supported / token not registered in Snowbridge
error_patterns.cause: Only tokens registered with the Snowbridge gateway contract on Ethereum can be bridged.
error_patterns.resolution: Check supported tokens at https://bridge.polkadot.io/. For unsupported tokens, the Snowbridge gateway must register the token via governance before it can be bridged.

supplementary_context.description: Load these pages for parachain XCM transfers, fee estimation, or to understand how bridged assets behave on Polkadot Hub.

pages.title: Transfer Assets Between Parachains
pages.relevance: How to transfer assets between Polkadot parachains using ParaSpell XCM — use when tokens are already in the Polkadot ecosystem.

pages.title: XCM Fee Estimation
pages.relevance: Understanding XCM fees on the Polkadot side when bridged assets arrive on Polkadot Hub.

examples.scenario: Common scenario: bridge 0.001 WETH from Ethereum to Polkadot Hub
examples.user_says: Bridge some WETH from Ethereum mainnet to my Polkadot Hub address
actions: Scaffold 'snowbridge-transfer/' as an ESM Node.js project
Install @paraspell/sdk, ethers, dotenv
Create .env; ask user to fill PRIVATE_KEY, ETH_RPC_URL, DEST_POLKADOT_ADDRESS without asking in chat
Create bridge.ts with the Snowbridge transfer script
Run npx tsx bridge.ts and capture transaction hash
Wait 15-30 minutes and verify token arrival at destination on Polkadot Hub Asset Hub
examples.result: WETH bridged from Ethereum mainnet; transaction confirmed on Ethereum; tokens arrive at Polkadot Hub address after relay processing

examples.scenario: Edge case: no tokens arrived after 60 minutes
examples.user_says: I submitted the Snowbridge transaction but my WETH hasn't appeared on Polkadot Hub after an hour
actions: Ask user for their Ethereum transaction hash
Direct to https://bridge.polkadot.io/ to check bridge relay status for that hash
If status is Pending, advise waiting up to 2 hours total
If status is Failed, check Polkadot Discord #snowbridge for relay outages
Warn user not to resubmit without confirming tokens are not in transit
examples.result: User identifies relay status and decides next steps without risking double-submission

skills.title: Convert Assets on Asset Hub via AMM
skills.description: Demonstrates the AssetConversion AMM pallet on Polkadot Hub Asset Hub — creating token pools, managing liquidity, and swapping assets via the Polkadot.js Apps browser UI. Use when a user wants to create a liquidity pool between DOT and a local or foreign asset, add or withdraw liquidity, or swap tokens via the AssetConversion extrinsics. Trigger phrases: 'convert assets Asset Hub', 'create liquidity pool Polkadot', 'swap tokens AMM', 'AssetConversion pallet', 'add liquidity Polkadot Hub'. Requires DOT and a second fungible asset on Asset Hub. All workflow steps use Polkadot.js Apps browser UI — no CLI tooling required.

runtime: A modern web browser (Chrome or Firefox recommended)
network: Polkadot Hub Asset Hub — wss://polkadot-asset-hub-rpc.polkadot.io (mainnet) or wss://asset-hub-paseo-rpc.dotters.network (Paseo TestNet)
tokens: DOT on Polkadot Hub Asset Hub for pool creation deposit and as one side of the pair
A second fungible asset (local integer-ID or foreign multilocation asset) already registered on Asset Hub
wallet: Polkadot.js browser extension with an account funded with DOT and the second asset on Asset Hub

steps.action: Open Polkadot.js Apps and connect to Asset Hub
steps.description: Navigate to https://polkadot.js.org/apps/ in the browser. In the top-left network selector, choose 'Polkadot Hub' under Polkadot (wss://polkadot-asset-hub-rpc.polkadot.io). For Paseo TestNet use wss://asset-hub-paseo-rpc.dotters.network. Verify the connected account in Accounts has DOT and the second asset. If testing with Paseo, get testnet PAS at https://faucet.polkadot.io/.

Navigate to Developer > Extrinsics. Select the 'assetConversion' pallet and 'createPool' extrinsic. Fill in:
- asset1: the first asset MultiLocation or AssetId (for native DOT use { parents: 1, interior: Here })
- asset2: the second asset (local integer AssetId or foreign MultiLocation)
Click 'Submit Transaction' and sign. A pool creation deposit is charged from the account. Verify the pool exists at Developer > Chain State > assetConversion > pools.

Navigate to Developer > Extrinsics > assetConversion > addLiquidity. Fill in:
- asset1 and asset2: same identifiers as pool creation
- amount1Desired: amount of asset1 to provide in smallest units
- amount2Desired: amount of asset2 to provide in smallest units
- amount1Min, amount2Min: set to 95% of desired amounts to allow for slippage
- mintTo: account address to receive LP tokens
Submit and sign. Confirm LP token receipt at Developer > Chain State > assetConversion > poolAssets > account.

Navigate to Developer > Extrinsics > assetConversion > swapExactTokensForTokens. Fill in:
- path: array of asset identifiers for the swap route (e.g., [asset1, asset2])
- amountIn: exact amount of asset1 to sell in smallest units
- amountOutMin: minimum amount of asset2 to receive (set to ~95% of expected output)
- sendTo: destination account for the received tokens
- keepAlive: true to prevent existential deposit issues
Submit and sign. Verify updated balances at Accounts > your account.

Navigate to Developer > Extrinsics > assetConversion > removeLiquidity. Fill in:
- asset1 and asset2: same identifiers as pool creation
- lpTokenBurn: amount of LP tokens to burn
- amount1MinReceive, amount2MinReceive: minimum amounts to receive (~95% of expected)
- withdrawTo: account address for withdrawn assets
Submit and sign. Confirm withdrawn amounts at Accounts > your account.

error_patterns.pattern: Dispatch Error: assetConversion.PoolNotFound
error_patterns.cause: Attempting to add liquidity or swap before the pool has been created.
error_patterns.resolution: Complete step 2 (createPool) first. Verify the pool exists at Developer > Chain State > assetConversion > pools before calling addLiquidity or swap extrinsics.

error_patterns.pattern: Dispatch Error: assetConversion.InsufficientLiquidity
error_patterns.cause: The pool has insufficient reserves to fulfill the swap at the specified slippage tolerance.
error_patterns.resolution: Reduce the swap amount or increase pool reserves by adding more liquidity. Check current reserves at Developer > Chain State > assetConversion > pools.

error_patterns.pattern: Dispatch Error: assetConversion.SlippageBoundsExceeded
error_patterns.cause: The swap output was less than amountOutMin, or add-liquidity amounts violated the current price ratio.
error_patterns.resolution: Increase slippage tolerance (lower amountOutMin) or resubmit after the price stabilizes.

error_patterns.pattern: Insufficient balance to pay pool creation deposit
error_patterns.cause: The account creating the pool lacks the required DOT deposit.
error_patterns.resolution: Fund the account with additional DOT. Query the exact deposit amount at Developer > Chain State > assetConversion > poolSetupFee.

supplementary_context.description: Load these pages for asset registration context or to understand Asset Hub fungible asset mechanics.

pages.title: Register a Local Asset
pages.relevance: How to register a new local integer-ID asset on Asset Hub before using it in an AssetConversion pool.

pages.title: Register a Foreign Asset
pages.relevance: How to register a foreign cross-chain asset on Asset Hub, which can then be used in an AssetConversion pool.

examples.scenario: Common scenario: create a DOT/USDT liquidity pool on Asset Hub
examples.user_says: Create a liquidity pool between DOT and USDT on Polkadot Hub Asset Hub
actions: Open Polkadot.js Apps connected to Asset Hub mainnet
Navigate to Developer > Extrinsics > assetConversion > createPool with DOT and USDT asset IDs
Submit and sign the pool creation transaction
Navigate to assetConversion > addLiquidity with desired DOT and USDT amounts and 95% slippage tolerance
Confirm LP tokens received at Developer > Chain State > assetConversion > poolAssets > account
examples.result: Liquidity pool created; LP tokens minted to account; pool visible in assetConversion > pools chain state

examples.scenario: Edge case: swap fails with SlippageBoundsExceeded error
examples.user_says: My asset swap failed with a slippage error
actions: Query current pool reserves at Developer > Chain State > assetConversion > pools
Recalculate expected output based on current reserves using constant product formula (x * y = k)
Resubmit swapExactTokensForTokens with amountOutMin set to 90% of the recalculated expected output
examples.result: Swap retried with adjusted slippage tolerance; transaction succeeds

skills.title: Register a Foreign Asset on Polkadot Hub
skills.description: Registers a cross-chain asset from another parachain on Polkadot Hub Asset Hub using the foreignAssets pallet. Covers generating the encoded forceCreate call data on Asset Hub, funding the source parachain sovereign account on Asset Hub, and dispatching the registration XCM from the source parachain. Use when a parachain team needs their token available on Polkadot Hub for swapping or bridging. Trigger phrases: 'register foreign asset Polkadot Hub', 'foreignAssets forceCreate', 'XCM asset registration', 'list token Asset Hub', 'register cross-chain asset'. Requires sudo or governance rights on the source parachain. All steps use Polkadot.js Apps — no CLI required.

runtime: A modern web browser (Chrome or Firefox recommended)
network: Polkadot Hub Asset Hub RPC (wss://polkadot-asset-hub-rpc.polkadot.io)
Source parachain RPC endpoint where the asset originates
tokens: DOT on Polkadot Hub Asset Hub sovereign account for the source parachain (to pay XCM execution fees)
Native tokens on source parachain to pay fees for the XCM send extrinsic
wallet: Polkadot.js browser extension with an account that has sudo or governance rights on the source parachain

steps.description: Determine the canonical MultiLocation for the asset to register. For a parachain's native token the MultiLocation is typically: { parents: 1, interior: { X1: [{ Parachain: <para-id> }] } }. For tokens in the source parachain's assets pallet: { parents: 1, interior: { X3: [{ Parachain: <para-id> }, { PalletInstance: <assets-pallet-id> }, { GeneralIndex: <asset-id> }] } }. Verify the MultiLocation with the parachain team and record it — it will be the assetId parameter in step 2.

steps.action: Encode the foreignAssets.forceCreate call on Asset Hub
steps.description:
In Polkadot.js Apps connected to Asset Hub (wss://polkadot-asset-hub-rpc.polkadot.io), navigate to Developer > Extrinsics. Select 'foreignAssets' pallet > 'forceCreate' extrinsic. Fill in:
- id: the MultiLocation from step 1
- owner: account that will own the registered asset (e.g., sovereign account of source parachain, or a designated admin)
- isSufficient: false (foreign assets should not be sufficient for account existence)
- minBalance: 1 (or higher as specified by the asset team)
Do NOT submit. Click the clipboard icon to copy the encoded call data hex. Record this hex — it will be used as the Transact call in step 4.

steps.action: Fund the source parachain sovereign account on Asset Hub
steps.description: The XCM execution on Asset Hub deducts fees from the source parachain's sovereign account on Asset Hub. The sovereign account address is derived from the parachain ID using the 'sibl' prefix for sibling chains. Use the Polkadot.js Apps Developer > JavaScript console or https://polkadot.subscan.io/tools/format_transform to derive the address from the para ID. In Polkadot.js Apps on Asset Hub, verify the sovereign account balance at Accounts. If insufficient, transfer at least 0.5 DOT to the sovereign account from a funded account before proceeding.

steps.action: Dispatch the registration XCM from the source parachain
steps.description:
Switch Polkadot.js Apps to the source parachain endpoint. Navigate to Developer > Extrinsics > polkadotXcm (or xcmPallet) > send. Configure:
- dest: { V3: { parents: 1, interior: { X1: [{ Parachain: 1000 }] } } } (Asset Hub para ID 1000 on Polkadot mainnet; 1000 on Paseo TestNet)
- message: V3 XCM with instructions: (1) UnpaidExecution { weight_limit: Unlimited, check_origin: None }, (2) Transact { origin_kind: Superuser, require_weight_at_most: { ref_time: 1000000000, proof_size: 65536 }, call: <hex from step 2> }, (3) RefundSurplus, (4) DepositAsset targeting the sovereign account for fee refunds
Submit and sign with a sudo or governance account on the source parachain.

steps.action: Verify the foreign asset is registered
steps.description: After 1-2 blocks, switch Polkadot.js Apps back to Asset Hub. Navigate to Developer > Chain State > foreignAssets > asset and query with the MultiLocation from step 1. A successful registration shows owner, minBalance, and isSufficient. If the asset is not found, check XCM result events on the source parachain (xcmPallet events) and on Asset Hub (system > ExtrinsicFailed or foreignAssets > Created events).

error_patterns.pattern: XCM Barrier not passed / NotHoldingFees
error_patterns.cause: The sovereign account on Asset Hub has insufficient DOT to pay XCM execution fees, or the XCM message lacks a fee payment instruction.
error_patterns.resolution: Fund the sovereign account with additional DOT (step 3). If the barrier error persists, add a BuyExecution instruction before Transact to explicitly purchase execution from sovereign account DOT.

error_patterns.pattern: foreignAssets.AlreadyExists on Asset Hub
error_patterns.cause: An asset with this MultiLocation is already registered on Asset Hub.
error_patterns.resolution: Query Developer > Chain State > foreignAssets > asset with the target MultiLocation before registration. If it already exists, skip registration or update metadata with foreignAssets.forceSetMetadata instead.

error_patterns.pattern: XCM Transact decoded incorrectly / wrong runtime version
error_patterns.cause: Encoded call data was generated against a different Asset Hub runtime version, or the extrinsic index changed after a runtime upgrade.
error_patterns.resolution: Re-generate the encoded call data in step 2 against the current Asset Hub node. Verify by decoding it: Developer > Extrinsics > Decode and paste the hex — it should resolve to foreignAssets.forceCreate.

supplementary_context.description: Load these pages for XCM message construction, sovereign account derivation, or asset mechanics.

pages.title: Transfer Assets Between Parachains
pages.relevance: How to transfer assets between parachains via XCM — useful for understanding how foreign assets flow after registration.

pages.title: Convert Assets on Asset Hub
pages.relevance: How to create AMM liquidity pools on Asset Hub — foreign assets can be used in AssetConversion pools after registration.

pages.title: Interoperability
pages.relevance: XCM instruction set reference, MultiLocation syntax, and sovereign account derivation.

examples.scenario: Common scenario: register a parachain native token on Asset Hub
examples.user_says: Register our parachain's token as a foreign asset on Polkadot Hub Asset Hub
actions: Determine the token MultiLocation based on the parachain ID
In Polkadot.js Apps on Asset Hub, build foreignAssets.forceCreate and copy encoded call hex
Fund the source parachain sovereign account on Asset Hub with at least 0.5 DOT
In Polkadot.js Apps on source parachain, dispatch XCM send with Transact wrapping the forceCreate hex
Verify foreignAssets.asset entry on Asset Hub after XCM execution
examples.result: Foreign asset registered on Asset Hub; visible at Developer > Chain State > foreignAssets > asset

examples.scenario: Edge case: XCM Transact failed with fee barrier error
examples.user_says: The XCM registration failed with a barrier or fee error on Asset Hub
actions: Check sovereign account DOT balance on Asset Hub at Developer > Chain State > system > account
Transfer 1 DOT to the sovereign account if balance is insufficient
Add BuyExecution instruction before Transact in the XCM message
Re-encode and resubmit the XCM from the source parachain
examples.result: XCM passes the barrier; Transact executes forceCreate; foreign asset registered

skills.title: Register a Local Asset on Polkadot Hub
skills.description: Creates a new local fungible asset with an integer asset ID on Polkadot Hub Asset Hub using the Assets pallet, accessible through the Polkadot.js Apps Network-Assets interface. Covers asset creation, setting metadata (name, symbol, decimals), minting initial supply, and verification. Use when a team needs to create a new token on Polkadot Hub's native asset pallet — stablecoins, project tokens, or test assets. Trigger phrases: 'create local asset Polkadot Hub', 'register asset Asset Hub', 'Assets pallet create', 'mint token Asset Hub', 'new fungible asset Polkadot'. Requires a DOT-funded account for the creation deposit. All steps use Polkadot.js Apps — no CLI required.

runtime: A modern web browser (Chrome or Firefox recommended)
network: Polkadot Hub Asset Hub — wss://polkadot-asset-hub-rpc.polkadot.io (mainnet) or wss://asset-hub-paseo-rpc.dotters.network (Paseo TestNet)
tokens: DOT on Polkadot Hub Asset Hub to pay the asset creation deposit (query Developer > Chain State > assets > assetDeposit for the current amount)
wallet: Polkadot.js browser extension with an account funded with DOT on Asset Hub

steps.action: Open Polkadot.js Apps and navigate to Network > Assets
steps.description: Navigate to https://polkadot.js.org/apps/?rpc=wss://polkadot-asset-hub-rpc.polkadot.io in the browser. For Paseo TestNet use wss://asset-hub-paseo-rpc.dotters.network. Click 'Network' in the top navigation and select 'Assets'. The Assets page lists all existing assets on Asset Hub. Click '+ Create' to begin asset creation.

steps.action: Fill in asset creation form — Step 1: Asset details
steps.description:
In the asset creation wizard, fill in the first step:
- asset id: a unique integer ID not already in use (verify on the Assets page). Well-known IDs to avoid: 1984 (USDT), 1337 (USDC). Choose a number that fits your project (e.g., 100-999 for project tokens)
- creator: connected wallet account (auto-populated)
- default admin: account controlling asset operations (typically same as creator)
Click 'Next' to proceed.

steps.action: Fill in asset creation form — Step 2: Metadata
steps.description:
In step 2 of the wizard, fill in the asset metadata:
- name: full token name (e.g., 'My Project Token')
- symbol: short ticker symbol (e.g., 'MPT')
- decimals: number of decimal places (6 for stablecoins, 10 for DOT-compatible, 18 for ETH-compatible)
- minimum balance: existential deposit for this asset; set to 1 unless specified otherwise
Verify the total estimated deposit shown in the form. Ensure the account has enough DOT.

steps.action: Submit and sign the asset creation transaction
steps.description: Review all details. Click 'Create' then 'Sign and Submit' in the confirmation dialog. Sign with the Polkadot.js extension. The creation deposit is charged from the creator's DOT balance. Wait for the transaction to be included in a block (10-30 seconds on mainnet). The new asset should appear in the Assets list with status 'Active'. Click the asset ID to verify the metadata (name, symbol, decimals, owner).

To mint tokens, navigate to Developer > Extrinsics > assets > mint. Fill in:
- id: the asset ID from step 2
- beneficiary: account to receive the minted tokens
- amount: number of tokens in smallest units (e.g., for 1000 tokens at 6 decimals, enter 1000000000)
Submit and sign with the asset issuer account. Verify the balance at Developer > Chain State > assets > account by querying the asset ID and beneficiary address.

error_patterns.pattern: Dispatch Error: assets.InUse / assets.AlreadyExists
error_patterns.cause: The asset ID entered is already taken by another asset on Asset Hub.
error_patterns.resolution: Check the Assets page for existing asset IDs and choose a different unused integer ID. Well-known reserved IDs include 1984 (USDT) and 1337 (USDC) — avoid those ranges.

error_patterns.pattern: Dispatch Error: assets.DepositNeeded / BalanceLow
error_patterns.cause: The creator account does not have enough DOT to pay the asset creation deposit.
error_patterns.resolution: Fund the account with additional DOT. Query the exact deposit amount at Developer > Chain State > assets > assetDeposit.

error_patterns.pattern: Dispatch Error: assets.NoPermission when minting
error_patterns.cause: The account attempting to mint is not the designated issuer for the asset.
error_patterns.resolution: Mint must be signed by the asset issuer account. Check the issuer address at Developer > Chain State > assets > asset for your asset ID, then switch to that account in the Polkadot.js extension.

skills.title: Set Up a Polkadot Relay Chain Full Node
skills.description: Installs and starts a Polkadot relay chain full node using one of four methods: pre-built binary, Docker, Snap, or compiled from source. Covers binary selection and installation, pruned vs archive sync mode, systemd service configuration, and sync verification. Use when running a self-hosted Polkadot relay chain node for RPC access, validator setup, or personal infrastructure. Trigger phrases: 'run Polkadot relay chain node', 'install polkadot full node', 'polkadot archive node', 'relay chain sync', 'self-hosted Polkadot node'. Requires a Linux server with 100 GB+ SSD (pruned) or 2+ TB SSD (archive). No tokens required. Companion skills: set-up-relay-chain-bootnode, set-up-polkadot-validator-node.

runtime: 64-bit Linux server (Ubuntu 22.04+ or similar) with systemd
For compile-from-source: Rust toolchain and SDK build dependencies (see https://docs.polkadot.com/parachains/install-polkadot-sdk.md)
Disk: 100+ GB SSD for pruned node; 2+ TB SSD for archive node
RAM: minimum 8 GB; 16 GB recommended for archive nodes; 4+ CPU cores for compilation
network: Open TCP port 30333 for P2P (required for peer discovery)
Port 9944 for WebSocket RPC (bind to localhost for private nodes; open only if public RPC is needed)
Stable broadband for initial chain sync

steps.action: Choose installation method and sync mode
steps.description:
Choose an installation method before proceeding:
(A) Pre-built binary — fastest setup; continue with step 2
(B) Docker — containerized with managed restarts; skip to step 3
(C) Snap — managed updates on Ubuntu; skip to step 4
(D) Compile from source — requires https://docs.polkadot.com/parachains/install-polkadot-sdk.md prerequisites; skip to step 5

Also choose sync mode:
- Pruned (--state-pruning=1000 --blocks-pruning=1000): retains last 1000 blocks; ~100 GB disk; recommended for most use cases
- Archive (no pruning flags): full historical state; 2+ TB disk; required for historical queries
- Warp sync (--sync=warp): downloads state snapshot first, fastest initial sync; recommended for pruned nodes

Download the stable polkadot release from GitHub. All three binaries are required — polkadot-execute-worker and polkadot-prepare-worker are needed for parachain validation. Replace 'polkadot-stable2412' with the latest stable release tag from https://github.com/paritytech/polkadot-sdk/releases. Verify: polkadot --version. Proceed to step 6.

Pull the stable polkadot Docker image and run the relay chain node. Replace '/var/lib/polkadot' with your preferred data directory. For archive mode, remove --state-pruning and --blocks-pruning. Add -p 9944:9944 to expose RPC externally. Monitor: docker logs -f polkadot-relay. Proceed to step 7.

Install polkadot via snap on Ubuntu. The snap manages updates automatically and includes all required binaries. Verify: polkadot --version. Start: sudo snap start polkadot. For custom startup flags, edit the snap service configuration. Proceed to step 7.

commands: cd polkadot-sdk && cargo build --release -p polkadot -p polkadot-execute-worker -p polkadot-prepare-worker
steps.description: Clone the polkadot-sdk repo and compile. Requires Rust toolchain and SDK dependencies — complete https://docs.polkadot.com/parachains/install-polkadot-sdk.md first. Replace 'stable2412' with the target stable branch. Compilation takes 20-45 minutes on 4+ cores. Verify: polkadot --version. Proceed to step 6.

steps.action: Create a systemd service (Methods A, C, D)
steps.description:
Create a polkadot system user and data directory, then configure systemd:

sudo useradd --system --no-create-home --shell /usr/sbin/nologin polkadot
sudo mkdir -p /var/lib/polkadot && sudo chown polkadot:polkadot /var/lib/polkadot

Create /etc/systemd/system/polkadot.service:

[Unit]
Description=Polkadot Relay Chain Node
After=network.target

[Service]
User=polkadot
ExecStart=/usr/local/bin/polkadot --base-path=/var/lib/polkadot --chain=polkadot --sync=warp --state-pruning=1000 --blocks-pruning=1000 --rpc-port=9944 --rpc-addr=127.0.0.1:9944
Restart=always
RestartSec=10

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target

For archive mode, remove --state-pruning and --blocks-pruning. For public RPC, replace --rpc-addr=127.0.0.1:9944 with --rpc-external. Then enable and start:

sudo systemctl daemon-reload && sudo systemctl enable polkadot && sudo systemctl start polkadot

For systemd: sudo journalctl -fu polkadot. For Docker: docker logs -f polkadot-relay. The node is fully synced when system_syncState currentBlock equals highestBlock and system_health isSyncing is false. Warp sync completes in minutes; archive sync can take days.

error_patterns.pattern: polkadot: error while loading shared libraries / polkadot-execute-worker: not found
error_patterns.cause: The polkadot binary requires polkadot-execute-worker and polkadot-prepare-worker to be installed in the same $PATH directory.
error_patterns.resolution: Ensure all three binaries are in the same directory with chmod 755. Verify: which polkadot-execute-worker should return a path. Reinstall from the same release version if workers are missing.

error_patterns.pattern: Peers count stays at 0 after 10 minutes
error_patterns.cause: P2P port 30333 is blocked by a firewall or cloud security group.
error_patterns.resolution: Open TCP port 30333: sudo ufw allow 30333/tcp. For cloud VMs, add an inbound rule for port 30333 in the security group. Mainnet bootnodes are embedded in the chain spec — no manual bootnode configuration is needed.

error_patterns.pattern: Error: database version mismatch / RocksDB migration required
error_patterns.cause: The polkadot binary version is incompatible with the existing database.
error_patterns.resolution: Stop the service, purge the database: polkadot purge-chain --chain=polkadot -y, then restart. The node will re-sync from scratch. For archive nodes, consider downloading a fresh snapshot to avoid a full re-sync.

error_patterns.pattern: System disk usage growing unexpectedly fast
error_patterns.cause: Running in archive mode without pruning flags, or warp sync completed and the node is now downloading full state.
error_patterns.resolution: Add --state-pruning=1000 --blocks-pruning=1000 to the service ExecStart for a pruned node. Stop the service, purge the old database, restart with pruning flags. Check disk usage: du -sh /var/lib/polkadot.

examples.user_says: Run a Polkadot relay chain full node
actions: Download polkadot, polkadot-execute-worker, and polkadot-prepare-worker from GitHub stable release
Install all three binaries to /usr/local/bin with chmod 755
Create polkadot system user and /var/lib/polkadot data directory
Create /etc/systemd/system/polkadot.service with warp sync, pruning, and localhost RPC
Enable and start the service
Verify sync with system_syncState and system_health JSON-RPC queries

examples.scenario: Edge case: node starts but stays at 0 peers
examples.user_says: My relay chain node shows 0 peers and is not syncing
actions: Check firewall: sudo ufw status — port 30333 must be open for TCP
For cloud VMs, verify the security group allows inbound TCP port 30333
Confirm the node responds to RPC: curl http://localhost:9944
Check logs for 'Connecting to boot node' messages
If still 0 peers after 15 minutes with correct firewall, check Polkadot status at https://polkadot.io/ecosystem
examples.result: Firewall issue identified and resolved; node connects to peers and begins syncing

skills.title: Set Up Secure WebSocket (WSS) for a Relay Chain Node
skills.description: Adds a WSS (WebSocket Secure) reverse proxy in front of a Polkadot relay chain node so that wallets, dApps, and light clients can connect over wss:// instead of the unencrypted ws://. Covers installing nginx or Apache2, obtaining an SSL certificate with Let's Encrypt (certbot), writing the reverse proxy virtual host config, and verifying the WSS connection. Use when exposing a relay chain RPC endpoint publicly or when MetaMask or Polkadot.js Apps require a wss:// URL. Requires an existing running relay chain node (companion skill: set-up-relay-chain-full-node). Trigger phrases: 'secure websocket relay node', 'wss relay chain', 'nginx ssl polkadot node', 'let's encrypt polkadot rpc', 'configure wss endpoint'.

runtime: A running Polkadot relay chain node with RPC listening on localhost:9944 (see set-up-relay-chain-full-node)
A registered domain name with an A record pointing to the server's public IP (required for Let's Encrypt certificates)
nginx (apt install nginx) or Apache2 (apt install apache2) — choose one before starting
certbot for Let's Encrypt: apt install certbot python3-certbot-nginx (nginx) or python3-certbot-apache (Apache2)
network: TCP port 80 open (required by Let's Encrypt HTTP-01 challenge)
TCP port 443 open for WSS client connections
TCP port 9944 bound to localhost only on the Polkadot node (default)

commands: sudo apt-get install -y nginx certbot python3-certbot-nginx
steps.description: Install nginx and the Let's Encrypt certbot plugin. If you prefer Apache2 instead, run: sudo apt-get install -y apache2 certbot python3-certbot-apache. Choose one web server and use it for all subsequent steps. Verify nginx is running: sudo systemctl status nginx.

steps.action: Obtain an SSL certificate from Let's Encrypt
steps.description: Replace YOUR_DOMAIN with your actual domain name (e.g., rpc.mynode.example.com) and YOUR_EMAIL with a valid contact address. Certbot performs an HTTP-01 challenge on port 80 — ensure port 80 is open. On success, certificates are saved under /etc/letsencrypt/live/YOUR_DOMAIN/. For Apache2, replace --nginx with --apache. For self-signed certificates (no domain), skip this step and use openssl req -x509 -newkey rsa:4096 -keyout /etc/ssl/private/polkadot-selfsigned.key -out /etc/ssl/certs/polkadot-selfsigned.crt -days 365 -nodes -subj '/CN=localhost' instead; clients must disable certificate verification.

steps.action: Configure nginx reverse proxy for WSS

commands: curl -s --include --no-buffer -H 'Connection: Upgrade' -H 'Upgrade: websocket' -H 'Host: YOUR_DOMAIN' -H 'Origin: https://YOUR_DOMAIN' -H 'Sec-WebSocket-Key: dGhlIHNhbXBsZSBub25jZQ==' -H 'Sec-WebSocket-Version: 13' https://YOUR_DOMAIN/
steps.expected_output:
HTTP/1.1 101 Switching Protocols
Upgrade: websocket
Connection: Upgrade
steps.description: Replace YOUR_DOMAIN with your actual domain. A 101 Switching Protocols response confirms WSS is working. You can also test by connecting Polkadot.js Apps (https://polkadot.js.org/apps/) to wss://YOUR_DOMAIN — it should show the chain name and node peers in the top-left. If the curl command returns a 502, check that the Polkadot node RPC is listening on port 9944: sudo ss -tlnp | grep 9944.

Let's Encrypt certificates expire after 90 days. The certbot timer runs twice daily and renews certificates expiring within 30 days. The --dry-run verifies renewal will succeed. Confirm the timer is active: sudo systemctl status certbot.timer.

error_patterns.pattern: certbot: Error getting validation data / Failed authorization procedure
error_patterns.cause: Let's Encrypt HTTP-01 challenge cannot reach port 80 on your domain. Usually a firewall rule or the domain's DNS A record is wrong.
error_patterns.resolution: Verify: (1) sudo ufw allow 80/tcp; (2) curl http://YOUR_DOMAIN from an external machine returns nginx content; (3) dig YOUR_DOMAIN A returns the server's public IP. If the DNS hasn't propagated yet, wait up to 24 hours and retry.

error_patterns.pattern: nginx: [error] / 502 Bad Gateway when connecting to WSS
error_patterns.cause: nginx cannot reach the Polkadot RPC on localhost:9944 — either the node is not running or it is not listening on the expected port.
error_patterns.resolution: Check: sudo systemctl status polkadot (or equivalent service name). Verify port: sudo ss -tlnp | grep 9944. If the node is running but bound to 0.0.0.0 with a different port, update the proxy_pass URL in the nginx config to match.

error_patterns.pattern: WebSocket connection refused / ERR_SSL_PROTOCOL_ERROR in browser
error_patterns.cause: The nginx or Apache2 configuration has a syntax error, or the reverse proxy upgrade headers are missing.
error_patterns.resolution: Run sudo nginx -t (nginx) or sudo apachectl configtest (Apache2). Ensure the Upgrade and Connection headers are present in the location block. Reload after fixing: sudo systemctl reload nginx.

error_patterns.pattern: Self-signed certificate not trusted / NET::ERR_CERT_AUTHORITY_INVALID
error_patterns.cause: Browsers and some clients reject self-signed certificates.
error_patterns.resolution: For production, use Let's Encrypt (requires a domain). For local testing only, configure the client to accept the certificate: in Polkadot.js Apps, click 'Allow insecure connections' prompt. For programmatic clients, set rejectUnauthorized: false in TLS options (dev only — never in production).

examples.scenario: Common scenario: expose relay chain RPC over WSS with Let's Encrypt

Obtain Let's Encrypt certificate for the domain name
Create /etc/nginx/sites-available/polkadot-wss with reverse proxy config, substituting the domain name
Enable the site and reload nginx
Verify with curl WebSocket handshake against wss://YOUR_DOMAIN
examples.result: Polkadot RPC accessible at wss://YOUR_DOMAIN with a valid TLS certificate; wallets and Polkadot.js Apps can connect

examples.scenario: Edge case: no domain available, use self-signed certificate for local testing
examples.user_says: I don't have a domain name — can I still set up WSS locally?
actions: Generate a self-signed certificate with openssl
Create nginx config pointing to the self-signed cert paths

examples.result: WSS works on localhost with a self-signed cert; clients must explicitly trust the certificate — not suitable for production

skills.title: Onboard as a Polkadot Validator (Full Flow)
skills.description: Walks through the complete Polkadot validator onboarding workflow across three phases: (1) installing the polkadot binary and worker executables; (2) generating and registering session keys and the node identity key; (3) syncing the chain, bonding DOT via Polkadot.js Apps, submitting session keys on-chain, and configuring a systemd service for persistence. Use when setting up a new Polkadot validator node from scratch. Trigger phrases: 'become a Polkadot validator', 'onboard validator', 'set up validator node', 'bond DOT validator', 'register session keys', 'start validating Polkadot'. Prerequisite: a Linux server meeting hardware requirements (see set-up-polkadot-validator-node). Note: bonding and key submission steps involve Polkadot.js Apps GUI and must be delegated to the user.

runtime: 64-bit Linux server (Ubuntu 22.04+ recommended), minimum 8 CPU cores, 32 GB RAM, 1+ TB NVMe SSD
Polkadot binary, polkadot-execute-worker, and polkadot-prepare-worker installed (see set-up-relay-chain-full-node or compile from paritytech/polkadot-sdk stable branch)
network: TCP port 30333 open for P2P peer discovery
TCP port 9944 bound to localhost for RPC key operations
Stable internet connection for continuous chain sync
tokens: DOT tokens for bonding on Polkadot mainnet (minimum stake varies — check the minimum active nomination threshold)
Small amount of DOT in the stash account for transaction fees
wallet: Polkadot.js browser extension (https://polkadot.js.org/extension/) for stash and controller accounts
Stash account funded with bonding DOT amount plus fees
Controller account (can be same as stash) with a small DOT balance for extrinsic fees

steps.description: Download all three binaries from the latest stable polkadot-sdk release. Replace 'polkadot-stable2412' with the latest stable tag from https://github.com/paritytech/polkadot-sdk/releases. All three binaries must be in the same directory — polkadot-execute-worker and polkadot-prepare-worker are required for parachain validation. Verify: polkadot --version should print the version string.

steps.action: Create system user and data directory
steps.description: Create a dedicated non-login system user and a data directory for the validator. This limits the blast radius if the process is compromised.

steps.action: Start the node and wait for sync
steps.description: Replace MY_VALIDATOR_NAME with your chosen validator display name (visible on-chain and in dashboards). The --validator flag activates validator-specific logic. The node must be fully synced before key operations — this can take hours. Monitor sync: curl -s -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":1,"method":"system_syncState","params":[]}' http://localhost:9944 — wait until currentBlock equals highestBlock. For faster initial sync, add --sync=warp.

author_rotateKeys generates new session keys (BABE, GRANDPA, ImOnline, AuthorityDiscovery) and stores them in the node's keystore at /var/lib/polkadot/chains/polkadot/keystore/. Save the hex string from 'result' — this is your session key blob needed in step 6. Do not share the individual key files; only the public session key blob is submitted on-chain.

Generates a persistent libp2p node identity key at /var/lib/polkadot/node-key. This key defines your node's peer ID on the P2P network. The node-key file must be preserved — if lost, the node gets a new peer ID and loses its P2P reputation. Add --node-key-file /var/lib/polkadot/node-key to the startup command in step 7.

steps.action: Bond DOT and set session keys (GUI — delegate to user)
steps.description:
This step requires the Polkadot.js Apps GUI at https://polkadot.js.org/apps/#/staking/actions — delegate to the user:

1. Go to Network > Staking > Accounts > + Stash
2. Select your stash account, choose a value to bond (leave some DOT for fees), set reward destination
3. Click Bond and sign the transaction with Polkadot.js extension
4. After bonding confirms, click 'Set Session Key'
5. Paste the session key hex string from step 4 into the 'session key' field
6. Click Set Session Key and sign the transaction

Wait for both transactions to be included in a block before proceeding.

Create /etc/systemd/system/polkadot-validator.service:

[Unit]
Description=Polkadot Validator Node
After=network.target

[Service]
User=polkadot
ExecStart=/usr/local/bin/polkadot \
  --base-path=/var/lib/polkadot \
  --chain=polkadot \
  --validator \
  --name=MY_VALIDATOR_NAME \
  --node-key-file=/var/lib/polkadot/node-key \
  --rpc-port=9944 \
  --rpc-addr=127.0.0.1
Restart=always
RestartSec=10
LimitNOFILE=65536

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target

Replace MY_VALIDATOR_NAME. Then:
sudo systemctl daemon-reload && sudo systemctl enable polkadot-validator && sudo systemctl start polkadot-validator

Stop the manual node started in step 3 first: pkill polkadot

Verify the node is healthy (peers > 0, isSyncing false). Your validator will be in the 'waiting' set until selected in the next era. Check validator status at https://polkadot.js.org/apps/#/staking — look for your validator name. Check logs: sudo journalctl -fu polkadot-validator.

error_patterns.pattern: author_rotateKeys returns an error or empty result
error_patterns.cause: The node RPC is not running or not listening on localhost:9944, or the node is still initializing.
error_patterns.resolution: Check: curl http://localhost:9944 should return a connection (even if the response is malformed WS). Verify: sudo ss -tlnp | grep 9944. If the node is not running, start it with the command from step 3 and wait 30 seconds before retrying.

error_patterns.pattern: polkadot: error while loading shared libraries / polkadot-execute-worker: not found
error_patterns.cause: polkadot-execute-worker and polkadot-prepare-worker are missing from $PATH or not installed.
error_patterns.resolution: All three binaries must be in the same directory. Verify: which polkadot-execute-worker. Reinstall from the same GitHub release tag.

error_patterns.pattern: Validator stuck in 'waiting' set for multiple eras
error_patterns.cause: Bonded amount may be below the minimum active nomination threshold, or session keys were not correctly submitted.
error_patterns.resolution: Verify session keys are registered on-chain: Polkadot.js Apps > Developer > Chain State > session.nextKeys(YOUR_ACCOUNT) — should return the key blob. Check the minimum active nomination amount for the current era. Bond more DOT if below threshold.

error_patterns.pattern: Node reports 0 peers after 15 minutes
error_patterns.cause: P2P port 30333 is blocked by a firewall.
error_patterns.resolution: Open port 30333: sudo ufw allow 30333/tcp. For cloud VMs, add an inbound TCP rule for 30333 in the security group. Mainnet bootnodes are embedded in the chain spec — no manual bootnode configuration is needed.

pages.relevance: Binary installation and initial validator node configuration — Phase 1 of the onboarding flow.

pages.title: Validator Key Management
pages.relevance: Session key generation, node key management, and keystore operations — Phase 2 of the onboarding flow.

pages.title: Start Validating

pages.title: Set Up a Node
pages.relevance: Full relay chain node setup — validator node is a specialization of the full node setup.

examples.scenario: Common scenario: onboard a new Polkadot validator from scratch
examples.user_says: Set up a Polkadot validator node and start validating
actions: Download and install polkadot binary and worker executables from the latest stable release
Create polkadot system user and /var/lib/polkadot data directory
Start the node with --validator flag and wait for full chain sync
Generate session keys via author_rotateKeys RPC — save the hex result
Generate a persistent node identity key
Delegate GUI steps to user: bond DOT via Polkadot.js Apps, submit session key hex on-chain
Create and enable polkadot-validator systemd service with node-key-file flag
Verify health via system_health RPC
examples.result: Validator node running as a systemd service, session keys registered, and validator appearing in the waiting set on-chain

examples.scenario: Edge case: session key registration unclear — verify keys are set correctly
examples.user_says: How do I verify my session keys are registered on-chain?
actions: Open Polkadot.js Apps > Developer > Chain State
Query session.nextKeys(STASH_ACCOUNT_ADDRESS)
Compare returned hex blob with the output from author_rotateKeys in step 4
If the query returns None, the setKeys extrinsic was not submitted or did not finalize — repeat step 6
examples.result: Confirmed session keys are registered; validator will be included in the active set in the next era after bonding threshold is met

skills.title: Add an Existing Pallet to a Parachain Runtime
skills.description: Integrates an existing Polkadot SDK pallet into a local parachain runtime. Covers adding the pallet crate to Cargo.toml, implementing the pallet's Config trait in runtime/src/lib.rs, registering the pallet in the construct_runtime! macro, compiling the runtime, generating a chain spec, and running the parachain locally to verify the pallet is active. Use when extending a Polkadot SDK parachain template with standard SDK pallets (pallet-utility, pallet-multisig, pallet-proxy, etc.). Trigger phrases: 'add pallet to runtime', 'integrate pallet parachain', 'pallet-utility runtime', 'extend parachain runtime', 'construct_runtime pallet'. Prerequisite: working parachain template dev environment (see set-up-the-parachain-template). Final GUI verification uses Polkadot.js Apps.

runtime: Rust toolchain with the wasm32-unknown-unknown target (`rustup target add wasm32-unknown-unknown`)
A working parachain template checkout with a successful local build (see https://docs.polkadot.com/parachains/launch-a-parachain/set-up-the-parachain-template.md)
network: No external network required — all compilation and testing is local

steps.action: Identify the pallet version to use
steps.description: Check the existing Polkadot SDK version in your runtime's Cargo.toml. All new pallets must use the same version to avoid dependency conflicts. Look for entries like polkadot-sdk = { version = "0.X.0", ... } or per-crate pins. Note the version — you will use it in step 2. The docs use pallet-utility as the example pallet; substitute any SDK pallet name.

steps.action: Add the pallet dependency to runtime/Cargo.toml
steps.description:
Open runtime/Cargo.toml and add pallet-utility (or your target pallet) to the [dependencies] section. Use the same version and features pattern as existing pallets:

[dependencies]
...
pallet-utility = { version = "<SDK_VERSION>", default-features = false }

Also add pallet-utility to the std features list so it compiles correctly for native (non-WASM) targets:

[features]
std = [
    ...
    "pallet-utility/std",
]

Replace <SDK_VERSION> with the version you identified in step 1. After editing, run: cargo check -p runtime 2>&1 | head -20 — the output should not show dependency resolution errors.

steps.action: Implement the pallet's Config trait in runtime/src/lib.rs

steps.description: Build the full parachain node (includes the runtime WASM blob). This typically takes 5-15 minutes on first build. A successful build ends with: Compiling node-template vX.X.X ... Finished release [optimized] target(s). If compilation fails with 'the trait `Config` for `pallet_utility` is not satisfied', check that all required associated types are implemented in step 3.

steps.action: Generate a fresh chain spec and start the node locally
steps.description: Generate a development chain spec and start the node in --dev mode. The --tmp flag stores chain data in a temporary directory. Once the node is running and producing blocks (look for: Imported #1 in logs), open Polkadot.js Apps at https://polkadot.js.org/apps/?rpc=ws://localhost:9944 and navigate to Developer > Extrinsics. Verify that 'utility' appears in the pallet dropdown as a callable extrinsic module.

error_patterns.pattern: error[E0277]: the trait `pallet_utility::Config` is not implemented for `Runtime`
error_patterns.cause: The impl pallet_utility::Config for Runtime block is missing or has a typo in runtime/src/lib.rs.
error_patterns.resolution: Ensure the impl block is present and all required associated types are specified. Run cargo check -p runtime to see the full list of missing types. Each missing type will be listed as 'required by this bound in pallet_utility::Config'.

error_patterns.pattern: error[E0432]: unresolved import `pallet_utility`
error_patterns.cause: pallet-utility was not added to runtime/Cargo.toml, or it was added but Cargo has not re-resolved dependencies.
error_patterns.resolution: Add pallet-utility to runtime/Cargo.toml [dependencies] with the correct version and default-features = false. Run cargo build again — Cargo will fetch and compile the new dependency.

error_patterns.pattern: Utility pallet does not appear in Polkadot.js Apps extrinsics
error_patterns.cause: The pallet was registered in construct_runtime! but with the wrong name, or the node is running an old binary without the updated runtime.
error_patterns.resolution: Stop the node, rebuild with cargo build --release, regenerate the chain spec, and restart. Verify the construct_runtime! entry name exactly matches what you expect to see in the UI.

examples.scenario: Common scenario: add pallet-utility to a parachain runtime
examples.user_says: Add pallet-utility to my parachain runtime
actions: Check the existing Polkadot SDK version in runtime/Cargo.toml
Add pallet-utility dependency with matching version and default-features = false
Add pallet-utility/std to the std feature list
Implement pallet_utility::Config for Runtime in runtime/src/lib.rs with all required associated types
Add Utility: pallet_utility entry to construct_runtime!
Run cargo build --release
Generate chain spec and start node with --dev --tmp
Verify 'utility' appears in Polkadot.js Apps extrinsics
