examples.scenario: Edge case: compilation fails with missing associated type in Config
examples.user_says: I get a compiler error about missing associated types in my Config impl
actions: Read the full compiler error — each missing type is listed with 'required by this bound'
Add each missing associated type to the impl block
Refer to the pallet's source (src/lib.rs) or docs for the expected type definitions
Re-run cargo build --release
examples.result: All required Config associated types satisfied; compilation succeeds

skills.title: Configure Multiple Instances of a Pallet in a Runtime
skills.description: Configures two independent instances of an instantiable Polkadot SDK pallet within a single parachain runtime. Uses pallet-collective as the example to demonstrate adding TechnicalCommittee (Instance1) and Council (Instance2) with separate membership and voting parameters. Covers identifying instantiable pallets, adding per-instance Cargo dependencies, implementing Config(Instance1) and Config(Instance2) traits, registering both instances in construct_runtime!, compiling, and verifying instance independence. Use when a runtime needs two separate governance bodies, two token pools, or any other scenario requiring two isolated instances of the same pallet logic. Trigger phrases: 'multiple pallet instances', 'two collective instances', 'pallet-collective council technical committee', 'instantiable pallet runtime'. Prerequisite: working parachain template (see add-existing-pallet-to-runtime).

runtime: Working parachain template with a successful build (see https://docs.polkadot.com/parachains/launch-a-parachain/set-up-the-parachain-template.md)
Familiarity with adding a single pallet to a runtime (see add-existing-pallet-to-runtime)
network: No external network required — local development only

steps.description: Not all pallets support multiple instances. An instantiable pallet uses a second generic type parameter (the Instance) in its Config trait definition, e.g.: pub trait Config<I: 'static = ()>: frame_system::Config. Check the pallet's source or documentation for this pattern. pallet-collective and pallet-membership are standard examples. If the pallet does not have the Instance generic, it cannot be instantiated multiple times.

Open runtime/Cargo.toml. A single pallet crate covers all instances — add it once:

[dependencies]
...
pallet-collective = { version = "<SDK_VERSION>", default-features = false }
pallet-membership = { version = "<SDK_VERSION>", default-features = false }

Replace <SDK_VERSION> with the version matching your other SDK pallets (check existing entries). Add both to the std feature list:

[features]
std = [
    ...
    "pallet-collective/std",
    "pallet-membership/std",
]

Verify: cargo check -p runtime 2>&1 | head -20

Build the parachain node. Compilation succeeds when both instance Config impls are complete and construct_runtime! entries are correct. Expected output: Finished release [optimized] target(s). If you see 'conflicting implementations of trait Config<Instance1>', check that each instance type alias (TechnicalCommitteeInstance, CouncilInstance) is distinct.

Generate a fresh dev chain spec and start the node. Open Polkadot.js Apps at https://polkadot.js.org/apps/?rpc=ws://localhost:9944. Navigate to Developer > Extrinsics — verify both 'technicalCommittee' and 'council' appear as separate callable modules. Navigate to Developer > Chain State and confirm both pallet storage namespaces exist independently.

error_patterns.pattern: error[E0119]: conflicting implementations of trait `pallet_collective::Config<Instance1>`
error_patterns.cause: Two impl blocks are targeting the same instance type — usually because both use the default instance () instead of Instance1/Instance2.
error_patterns.resolution: Ensure each impl block explicitly names a different instance: impl pallet_collective::Config<Instance1> and impl pallet_collective::Config<Instance2>. Type aliases (type TechnicalCommitteeInstance = pallet_collective::Instance1;) help clarify intent.

error_patterns.pattern: error: the pallet does not implement the Instance trait
error_patterns.cause: The pallet does not support instantiation — it lacks the I: 'static = () generic in its Config trait.
error_patterns.resolution: Only instantiable pallets (those with a second generic parameter) can be used this way. Check the pallet source or use a different pallet that supports multiple instances.

error_patterns.pattern: Only one instance visible in Polkadot.js Apps
error_patterns.cause: One construct_runtime! entry is missing or has a typo.
error_patterns.resolution: Verify both entries are present in construct_runtime! with distinct names and correct instance specifiers: TechnicalCommittee: pallet_collective::<Instance1> and Council: pallet_collective::<Instance2>. Rebuild and restart the node.

examples.scenario: Common scenario: add TechnicalCommittee and Council as two pallet-collective instances
examples.user_says: Add a TechnicalCommittee and a Council to my parachain runtime using pallet-collective
actions: Verify pallet-collective has the I: 'static = () generic — confirm it is instantiable
Add pallet-collective to runtime/Cargo.toml with default-features = false and add to std features
Implement pallet_collective::Config<Instance1> as TechnicalCommitteeInstance with committee-appropriate parameters
Implement pallet_collective::Config<Instance2> as CouncilInstance with council-appropriate parameters
Add TechnicalCommittee: pallet_collective::<Instance1> and Council: pallet_collective::<Instance2> to construct_runtime!
Run cargo build --release
Start node in --dev mode and verify both 'technicalCommittee' and 'council' appear in Polkadot.js Apps
examples.result: Two independent pallet-collective instances registered — TechnicalCommittee and Council operate with independent storage, membership, and voting parameters

examples.scenario: Edge case: runtime compiles but only one instance shows in the UI
examples.user_says: My runtime compiled but I only see one collective in Polkadot.js Apps
actions: Check construct_runtime! for both TechnicalCommittee and Council entries
Verify each entry uses the correct instance specifier: ::<Instance1> and ::<Instance2>
Rebuild with cargo build --release
Regenerate chain spec and restart node
examples.result: Both collective instances visible and callable in Polkadot.js Apps

skills.title: Create a Custom FRAME Pallet
skills.description: Guides through building a custom FRAME pallet from scratch using the Polkadot SDK. Uses a counter pallet (increment and decrement extrinsics with bounded storage) as the example to demonstrate all core FRAME patterns: pallet directory layout, Cargo.toml configuration, #[pallet::config], #[pallet::storage], #[pallet::event], #[pallet::error], #[pallet::call], and genesis config. After implementing the pallet, integrates it into the parachain runtime (Cargo dependency, Config impl, construct_runtime!), compiles, and runs locally. Use when building any new runtime module with on-chain state and extrinsics. Trigger phrases: 'create FRAME pallet', 'custom pallet from scratch', 'write a pallet', 'FRAME storage events extrinsics', 'build counter pallet'. Prerequisites: Polkadot SDK installed, parachain template set up. Verification step uses Polkadot.js Apps GUI.

runtime: Rust toolchain with wasm32-unknown-unknown target (rustup target add wasm32-unknown-unknown)
Working parachain template checkout with a successful build (see https://docs.polkadot.com/parachains/launch-a-parachain/set-up-the-parachain-template.md)
network: No external network required — all work is local

steps.action: Create the pallet directory and Cargo.toml
steps.description:
Create the pallet directory structure inside the parachain template workspace. The pallet name (counter) becomes the crate name. Create pallets/counter/Cargo.toml with the following content, replacing <SDK_VERSION> with the version matching your other workspace pallets:

[package]
name = "pallet-counter"
version = "0.1.0"
edition = "2021"

[dependencies]
codec = { package = "parity-scale-codec", version = "3", default-features = false, features = ["derive"] }
scale-info = { version = "2", default-features = false, features = ["derive"] }
frame-support = { version = "<SDK_VERSION>", default-features = false }
frame-system = { version = "<SDK_VERSION>", default-features = false }
frame-benchmarking = { version = "<SDK_VERSION>", default-features = false, optional = true }

[features]
default = ["std"]
std = [
    "codec/std",
    "scale-info/std",
    "frame-support/std",
    "frame-system/std",
]
runtime-benchmarks = ["frame-benchmarking/runtime-benchmarks"]

Also add pallet-counter to the workspace's root Cargo.toml members list:
members = [
    ...
    "pallets/counter",
]

Open runtime/Cargo.toml and add pallet-counter as a workspace dependency:

[dependencies]
...
pallet-counter = { path = "../pallets/counter", default-features = false }

Add to the std feature list:
[features]
std = [
    ...
    "pallet-counter/std",
]

Note: path = "../pallets/counter" is a relative path from the runtime/ directory to the pallet directory.

Build the entire parachain node including the new pallet. Expected: Finished release [optimized]. Then generate a dev chain spec and start the node. Once running, open Polkadot.js Apps at https://polkadot.js.org/apps/?rpc=ws://localhost:9944 and navigate to Developer > Extrinsics — 'counter' should appear with increment and decrement callables. Submit an increment extrinsic signed with Alice (//Alice) and verify the CounterValue storage changes via Developer > Chain State > counter.counterValue.

error_patterns.pattern: error[E0412]: cannot find type `ConstU32` in this scope
error_patterns.cause: ConstU32 is not imported in runtime/src/lib.rs.
error_patterns.resolution: Add to the use statement at the top of runtime/src/lib.rs: use frame_support::traits::ConstU32; (or verify it is already imported via frame_support::prelude::*). The parachain template runtime usually imports it — search for existing ConstU32 usages to confirm.

error_patterns.pattern: error[E0277]: the trait `pallet_counter::Config` is not implemented
error_patterns.cause: The impl pallet_counter::Config for Runtime block is missing or has a missing associated type.
error_patterns.resolution: Ensure the impl block is in runtime/src/lib.rs and all required types are specified. The Rust compiler lists each missing associated type — add them one by one.

error_patterns.pattern: error: failed to select a version for `pallet-counter` / could not find Cargo.toml
error_patterns.cause: The path in runtime/Cargo.toml does not match the actual pallet directory location.
error_patterns.resolution: Verify the relative path: from runtime/, pallets/counter/ should be at ../pallets/counter. Run ls ../pallets/counter/Cargo.toml from inside the runtime/ directory to confirm the path exists.

error_patterns.pattern: CounterOverflow error when submitting increment
error_patterns.cause: The counter has reached MaxValue (100 by default).
error_patterns.resolution: Decrement first to free up space, or increase MaxValue in the runtime Config (type MaxValue = ConstU32<1000>;) and rebuild. This behavior is by design — the error confirms the overflow protection is working.

examples.scenario: Common scenario: build a counter pallet with increment and decrement extrinsics
examples.user_says: Create a custom FRAME pallet from scratch
actions: Create pallets/counter/src/ directory and lib.rs file
Write Cargo.toml with frame-support, frame-system, parity-scale-codec, and scale-info dependencies
Add pallet-counter to workspace root Cargo.toml members
Implement the pallet in lib.rs: Config trait, StorageValue, Event enum, Error enum, and two call extrinsics (increment/decrement) with overflow/underflow protection
Add pallet-counter path dependency to runtime/Cargo.toml
Implement pallet_counter::Config for Runtime with MaxValue = ConstU32<100>
Add Counter: pallet_counter to construct_runtime!
Run cargo build --release
Start node in --dev mode; submit increment extrinsic via Polkadot.js Apps; verify CounterValue storage
examples.result: Custom counter pallet deployed locally; increment and decrement extrinsics callable on-chain; CounterValue storage updates correctly with overflow protection

examples.scenario: Edge case: adding a genesis config to set an initial counter value
examples.user_says: How do I set the initial counter value to 10 at genesis?
actions: Add a #[pallet::genesis_config] struct and #[pallet::genesis_build] impl to the pallet's lib.rs to accept an initial_value
In the runtime's chain_spec.rs (or equivalent), include Counter: CounterConfig { initial_value: 10 } in the genesis state
Rebuild and regenerate the chain spec to include the genesis config
examples.result: Node starts with CounterValue pre-set to 10; visible in Developer > Chain State > counter.counterValue immediately after genesis

skills.title: Open an HRMP Channel Between Parachains
skills.description: Walks through the two-step HRMP channel setup between two user parachains. Step 1 (sender): fund the parachain sovereign account on the relay chain, construct an hrmpInitOpenChannel extrinsic, copy the encoded call data, and submit it via a polkadotXcm.send XCM message using sudo. Step 2 (receiver): repeat funding, construct hrmpAcceptOpenChannel, and submit via XCM. Use when enabling cross-chain messaging between two custom parachains on Polkadot. Trigger phrases: 'open HRMP channel', 'establish parachain communication', 'hrmpInitOpenChannel', 'XCM channel setup between parachains'. All steps use the Polkadot.js Apps GUI — no CLI. Repeat in reverse order for bidirectional communication.

network: A running relay chain (e.g. Rococo Local or Paseo) with at least two connected user parachains
RPC endpoint access to the relay chain and both parachains via Polkadot.js Apps
tokens: Funds in both parachain sovereign accounts on the relay chain to cover XCM transact fees
A wallet account with sudo or polkadotXcm.send privileges on each parachain

steps.action: Fund the sender parachain sovereign account on the relay chain
steps.description: In Polkadot.js Apps, connect to the relay chain. Navigate to Accounts and send funds to the parachain 2500 sovereign account. To compute the sovereign address: prefix 0x70617261 (b"para") + u32 SCALE encoding of the para ID (2500 = c4090000) + zero-padded to 32 bytes. For para 2500: 0x70617261c4090000000000000000000000000000000000000000000000000000 (SS58: 5Ec4AhPSY2GEE4VoHUVheqv5wwq2C1HMKa7c9fVJ1WKivX1Y). Use Substrate Utilities at https://www.shawntabrizi.com/substrate-js-utilities/ for other para IDs.

steps.action: Construct the hrmpInitOpenChannel extrinsic and copy encoded call data
steps.description: In Polkadot.js Apps, connect to the relay chain. Navigate to Developer > Extrinsics. Select the hrmp pallet and hrmpInitOpenChannel. Fill in: recipient = target para ID (e.g. 2600), proposedMaxCapacity = max pending messages (e.g. 8), proposedMaxMessageSize = max message size in bytes (e.g. 1024). Copy the encoded call data (example for para 2600: 0x3c00280a00000800000000001000). Save this value — used in step 3.

steps.action: Submit XCM message from sender parachain to initiate the channel request
steps.description: In Polkadot.js Apps, connect to parachain 2500. Navigate to Developer > Extrinsics. Use sudo to dispatch polkadotXcm.send with an XCM message containing: WithdrawAsset, BuyExecution, Transact (with the hrmpInitOpenChannel call data from step 2), RefundSurplus, DepositAsset (beneficiary = para 2500 sovereign account). Replace the call field with the call data from step 2. Click Submit Transaction and sign with the sudo key. The exact XCM parameters depend on your parachain's XCM configuration.

steps.action: Verify the channel request is registered on the relay chain
steps.description: In Polkadot.js Apps, connect to the relay chain. Navigate to Developer > Chain State. Select the hrmp pallet and query hrmpOpenChannelRequests (click +). Confirm an entry appears for parachain 2500 as sender and 2600 as recipient. The entry confirms the request was submitted and is awaiting acceptance.

steps.action: Fund the receiver parachain sovereign account on the relay chain
steps.description: In Polkadot.js Apps, connect to the relay chain. Transfer funds to the parachain 2600 sovereign account. Compute the sovereign address using the same method as step 1, substituting para ID 2600 (encoded as 280a0000).

steps.action: Construct the hrmpAcceptOpenChannel extrinsic and copy encoded call data
steps.description: In Polkadot.js Apps, connect to the relay chain. Navigate to Developer > Extrinsics. Select the hrmp pallet and hrmpAcceptOpenChannel. Fill in: sender = para ID of the requesting chain (2500). Copy the encoded call data (example: 0x3c01c4090000). Save this value — used in step 7.

steps.action: Submit XCM message from receiver parachain to accept the channel
steps.description: In Polkadot.js Apps, connect to parachain 2600. Repeat the XCM submission from step 3, but: replace the Transact call data with the hrmpAcceptOpenChannel call data from step 6, and in DepositAsset set the beneficiary to para 2600's sovereign account. Click Submit Transaction and sign.

steps.action: Verify the unidirectional channel is established
steps.description: In Polkadot.js Apps, connect to the relay chain. Navigate to Developer > Chain State. Select the hrmp pallet and query hrmpChannels (click +). Confirm an entry appears for the channel from para 2500 to para 2600. The channel is now open for one-directional message passing. To enable bidirectional communication, repeat the entire process with para 2600 as initiator and para 2500 as acceptor.

error_patterns.pattern: XCM message execution fails / no hrmpOpenChannelRequests entry after submission
error_patterns.cause: Insufficient funds in the parachain sovereign account on the relay chain, or the parachain's XCM configuration does not allow polkadotXcm.send from sudo.
error_patterns.resolution: Verify sovereign account balance via Developer > Chain State > system.account for the sovereign address. Top up with relay chain tokens. Confirm sudo can dispatch polkadotXcm.send on the parachain.

error_patterns.pattern: hrmpChannels query shows no channel after both steps
error_patterns.cause: The channel only becomes active at the start of the next relay chain session after both init and accept transactions are included.
error_patterns.resolution: Wait for a new session (observable via Developer > Chain State > session.currentIndex incrementing). Query hrmpChannels again after the session change.

error_patterns.pattern: Encoded call data is invalid or wrong extrinsic submitted
error_patterns.cause: The call data from step 2 or step 6 was entered incorrectly, or the para IDs were swapped.
error_patterns.resolution: Re-encode the extrinsic in Polkadot.js Apps with the correct parameters. For hrmpInitOpenChannel (para 2600, capacity 8, size 1024) the example call data is 0x3c00280a00000800000000001000; for hrmpAcceptOpenChannel (sender 2500) it is 0x3c01c4090000. If your para IDs differ, re-encode from scratch.

supplementary_context.description: Load these pages for XCM conceptual background or the simpler system-parachain channel variant.

pages.title: Get Started with XCM
pages.relevance: XCM concepts (asynchronous, absolute, asymmetric, agnostic) and the WithdrawAsset/BuyExecution/Transact/DepositAsset instruction pattern used in HRMP channel setup messages.

pages.title: Opening HRMP Channels with System Parachains
pages.relevance: Simpler single-message alternative when the target is a system parachain like Asset Hub.

examples.scenario: Common scenario: open a unidirectional HRMP channel from parachain 2500 to parachain 2600
examples.user_says: Open an HRMP channel between parachain 2500 and parachain 2600
actions: Fund para 2500 sovereign account on relay chain via Polkadot.js Apps
Encode hrmpInitOpenChannel (recipient=2600, maxCapacity=8, maxMessageSize=1024) and copy call data
Submit polkadotXcm.send from para 2500 with Transact = hrmpInitOpenChannel call data
Verify hrmpOpenChannelRequests entry on relay chain
Fund para 2600 sovereign account on relay chain
Encode hrmpAcceptOpenChannel (sender=2500) and copy call data
Submit polkadotXcm.send from para 2600 with Transact = hrmpAcceptOpenChannel call data
Verify hrmpChannels shows 2500→2600 channel after next session
examples.result: Unidirectional HRMP channel from para 2500 to para 2600 is active after the next session.

examples.scenario: Edge case: enable bidirectional communication between two parachains
examples.user_says: How do I enable two-way messaging between parachain 2500 and 2600?
actions: Complete the full channel setup with 2500 as initiator and 2600 as acceptor (steps 1-8)
Repeat the process with roles reversed: 2600 initiates hrmpInitOpenChannel (recipient=2500), 2500 accepts hrmpAcceptOpenChannel (sender=2600)
Verify hrmpChannels shows both 2500→2600 and 2600→2500 entries
examples.result: Both channels are active; messages flow in both directions between the parachains.

skills.title: Open an HRMP Channel with a System Parachain
skills.description: Guides through the simplified one-step process for opening a bidirectional HRMP channel between a user parachain and a Polkadot system parachain. Unlike regular parachain channels that require a two-step request and acceptance, a single XCM message with the establish_channel_with_system extrinsic opens channels in both directions simultaneously. Use when your parachain needs to communicate with Asset Hub (para 1000), Bridge Hub, or other system chains. Trigger phrases: 'open channel with Asset Hub', 'HRMP channel system parachain', 'establish_channel_with_system', 'parachain to Asset Hub channel'. All steps use Polkadot.js Apps GUI — no CLI. Channels become active at the start of the next session.

network: A running relay chain with both the user parachain and a system parachain (e.g. Asset Hub, para 1000) connected
RPC endpoint access to the relay chain and user parachain via Polkadot.js Apps
tokens: Funds in the user parachain's sovereign account on the relay chain to cover XCM transact fees
A wallet account with sudo or polkadotXcm.send privileges on the user parachain

steps.action: Fund the user parachain sovereign account on the relay chain
steps.description: In Polkadot.js Apps, connect to the relay chain. Navigate to Accounts and send funds to the user parachain's sovereign account. To compute the sovereign address: prefix 0x70617261 (b"para") + u32 SCALE encoding of the para ID (e.g. 2500 = c4090000) + zero-padded to 32 bytes. For para 2500: 0x70617261c4090000000000000000000000000000000000000000000000000000 (SS58: 5Ec4AhPSY2GEE4VoHUVheqv5wwq2C1HMKa7c9fVJ1WKivX1Y). Use Substrate Utilities at https://www.shawntabrizi.com/substrate-js-utilities/ for other para IDs.

steps.action: Construct the establish_channel_with_system extrinsic and copy encoded call data
steps.description: In Polkadot.js Apps, connect to the relay chain. Navigate to Developer > Extrinsics. Select the hrmp pallet and establish_channel_with_system. Fill in: target_system_chain = para ID of the target system chain (e.g. 1000 for Asset Hub). Copy the encoded call data (example for para 1000: 0x3c0ae8030000). Save this value — used in step 3.

steps.action: Submit XCM message from the user parachain to the relay chain
steps.description: In Polkadot.js Apps, connect to the user parachain. Navigate to Developer > Extrinsics. Use sudo to dispatch polkadotXcm.send with an XCM message containing: WithdrawAsset, BuyExecution, Transact (with the establish_channel_with_system call data from step 2), RefundSurplus, DepositAsset (beneficiary = parachain sovereign account). Replace the call field with the encoded call data from step 2. Click Submit Transaction and sign with the sudo key.

steps.action: Wait for the next session and verify bidirectional channels
steps.description: After submission, two HrmpSystemChannelOpened events are emitted. Channels do not become active until the start of the next session. Monitor session.currentIndex on the relay chain via Developer > Chain State. Once the session increments, query hrmp.hrmpChannels to verify both entries: from system chain (1000) to user parachain (2500) and from 2500 to 1000. Both entries confirm bidirectional communication is established.
steps.expected_output: hrmpChannels query shows two entries: 1000→2500 and 2500→1000

error_patterns.pattern: No HrmpSystemChannelOpened events / XCM execution fails
error_patterns.cause: Insufficient funds in the parachain sovereign account, or polkadotXcm.send is not callable with sudo on this parachain.
error_patterns.resolution: Verify sovereign account balance via Developer > Chain State > system.account. Top up as needed. Check the parachain's XCM config to confirm sudo can dispatch polkadotXcm.send.

error_patterns.pattern: hrmpChannels query returns no entries after submission
error_patterns.cause: Channels are only activated at the start of the next relay chain session after submission.
error_patterns.resolution: Wait for the session to change (observable via session.currentIndex incrementing). Query hrmpChannels again after the session increment.

error_patterns.pattern: establish_channel_with_system extrinsic not found in hrmp pallet
error_patterns.cause: The relay chain runtime may not include this extrinsic, or the target_system_chain para ID is incorrect.
error_patterns.resolution: Verify the relay chain runtime includes establish_channel_with_system in the hrmp pallet. Confirm the correct system chain para ID: Asset Hub on Polkadot mainnet and Paseo/Rococo testnet is 1000.

supplementary_context.description: Load these pages for XCM background or the two-step alternative for non-system parachains.

pages.title: Get Started with XCM

examples.scenario: Common scenario: open bidirectional HRMP channel from parachain 2500 to Asset Hub (para 1000)
examples.user_says: Open an HRMP channel between my parachain and Asset Hub
actions: Fund para 2500 sovereign account on relay chain via Polkadot.js Apps
Encode establish_channel_with_system (target_system_chain=1000) and copy call data 0x3c0ae8030000
Submit polkadotXcm.send from para 2500 with Transact = encoded call data
Wait for the next relay chain session
Query hrmp.hrmpChannels and confirm both 1000→2500 and 2500→1000 entries are present
examples.result: Bidirectional HRMP channel between para 2500 and Asset Hub (para 1000) is active after the next session.

examples.scenario: Edge case: channel not appearing after session change
examples.user_says: The hrmpChannels query is empty even after waiting for the next session
actions: Check that HrmpSystemChannelOpened events were emitted after XCM submission — if absent, the XCM failed
Verify the sovereign account balance was sufficient to pay XCM transact fees
Top up the sovereign account and re-submit the XCM message
Wait for the next session, then query hrmpChannels again
examples.result: Channels become visible after successful XCM execution and the session increment.

skills.title: Perform a Forkless Runtime Upgrade
skills.description: Guides through a complete forkless runtime upgrade cycle for a Polkadot SDK chain. You add a Root-only reset_counter dispatchable to a custom pallet, increment spec_version from 1 to 2 in the runtime VERSION constant, rebuild the Wasm binary with cargo build --release, and submit system.setCode via the Sudo pallet in Polkadot.js Apps. Use when adding features, fixing bugs, or upgrading runtime logic without a hard fork on a running local or testnet chain. Trigger phrases: 'runtime upgrade', 'forkless upgrade', 'update runtime', 'bump spec_version', 'submit setCode'. Prerequisite: a running chain with a custom pallet (see create-frame-pallet). Final submission step requires Polkadot.js Apps GUI — no CLI for that step.

runtime: Rust toolchain with wasm32-unknown-unknown target (rustup target add wasm32-unknown-unknown)
Parachain template built and running locally (see https://docs.polkadot.com/parachains/launch-a-parachain/set-up-the-parachain-template.md)
Custom pallet integrated into the runtime (see create-frame-pallet skill)
network: Local dev node running at ws://127.0.0.1:9944
Polkadot.js Apps access for the final upgrade submission step

steps.action: Start the chain and verify it is running at spec_version 1
steps.description: Start the Omni Node in development mode. Once producing blocks, open Polkadot.js Apps at https://polkadot.js.org/apps/?rpc=ws://127.0.0.1:9944. Confirm the header shows parachain-template-runtime/1 (spec_version 1). Keep the node running in the background.

steps.action: Add the reset_counter dispatchable to the custom pallet

steps.description: Build the project to confirm the new extrinsic compiles without errors. Fix any compiler errors before proceeding.

steps.action: Build the final runtime Wasm binary
steps.description: Build the complete runtime with the updated spec_version. After compilation verify the Wasm file exists: target/release/wbuild/parachain-template-runtime/parachain_template_runtime.compact.compressed.wasm. This is the file to upload in step 6.

steps.action: Submit the runtime upgrade via Polkadot.js Apps
steps.description: In Polkadot.js Apps connected to ws://127.0.0.1:9944: 1. Navigate to Developer > Extrinsics. 2. Select the sudo pallet, then sudo(call). 3. Select the system pallet, then setCode(code). 4. Click 'file upload' next to the code parameter. 5. Upload target/release/wbuild/parachain-template-runtime/parachain_template_runtime.compact.compressed.wasm. 6. Click Submit Transaction and sign with the sudo key (Alice for local dev chains). The upgrade takes effect immediately once included in a block.

In Polkadot.js Apps: 1. The header should now show parachain-template-runtime/2. 2. Navigate to Developer > Chain State > system > lastRuntimeUpgrade() and click + to confirm specVersion: 2. 3. Navigate to Developer > Extrinsics and select your customPallet — the new resetCounter function should appear. Test it: increment the counter, call resetCounter (requires sudo), verify the counter resets to 0 via Chain State.
steps.expected_output: parachain-template-runtime/2 in header; lastRuntimeUpgrade shows specVersion: 2

error_patterns.pattern: Runtime header still shows spec_version 1 after upgrade submission
error_patterns.cause: spec_version was not incremented before building — the node does not replace the runtime if spec_version is unchanged.
error_patterns.resolution: Edit runtime/src/lib.rs to set spec_version to a value higher than the current on-chain version. Rebuild with cargo build --release and resubmit via Polkadot.js Apps.

error_patterns.pattern: cargo build --release fails with type errors or missing function in the new extrinsic
error_patterns.cause: The new dispatchable uses a mismatched event variant or incorrect FRAME macro syntax.
error_patterns.resolution: Verify the call_index does not collide with existing extrinsics. Ensure the Event variant used in deposit_event exists in the pallet's Event enum. Confirm ensure_root is imported (frame_system::pallet_prelude::* covers it).

error_patterns.pattern: sudo pallet not found or Alice does not have sudo access
error_patterns.cause: The local dev chain genesis does not include the Sudo pallet with Alice as the sudo key.
error_patterns.resolution: Verify the Sudo pallet is in construct_runtime! and Alice (5GrwvaEF5zXb26Fz9rcQpDWS57CtERHpNehXCPcNoHGKutQY) is the sudo key via Developer > Chain State > sudo.key(). If not, restart with a new genesis that includes Sudo.

error_patterns.pattern: Wrong Wasm file path — upload fails or upgrade has no visible effect
error_patterns.cause: Multiple Wasm variants are generated; only the .compact.compressed.wasm is accepted for on-chain upgrades.
error_patterns.resolution: Upload exactly target/release/wbuild/parachain-template-runtime/parachain_template_runtime.compact.compressed.wasm. Uncompressed or non-compact variants are rejected.

supplementary_context.description: Load these pages for prerequisite setup context or for data migration requirements during upgrades.

pages.title: Create a Custom Pallet
pages.relevance: Required prerequisite: building the custom pallet whose extrinsic is extended in this skill.

pages.title: Storage Migrations
pages.relevance: When the runtime upgrade changes storage layout (type changes, key changes), a storage migration is required alongside the spec_version bump.

pages.title: Set Up the Polkadot SDK Parachain Template
pages.relevance: Setting up the parachain template that this upgrade workflow runs on.

examples.scenario: Common scenario: add a reset_counter extrinsic and perform a runtime upgrade
examples.user_says: Perform a runtime upgrade to add a resetCounter function to my custom pallet
actions: Start chain with polkadot-omni-node --dev and verify spec_version 1 in Polkadot.js Apps header
Add reset_counter (Root-only, sets CounterValue to 0) to pallet lib.rs inside #[pallet::call]
Run cargo build --release to verify compilation
Open runtime/src/lib.rs and change spec_version from 1 to 2 in the VERSION constant
Run cargo build --release to build the updated Wasm binary
In Polkadot.js Apps: Developer > Extrinsics > sudo > sudo(call) > system > setCode > upload parachain_template_runtime.compact.compressed.wasm > Submit Transaction
Verify header shows parachain-template-runtime/2 and lastRuntimeUpgrade confirms specVersion 2
Test resetCounter in Developer > Extrinsics
examples.result: Runtime upgraded to spec_version 2; resetCounter extrinsic available on-chain; counter resets to 0 when called with sudo.

examples.scenario: Edge case: submitted upgrade but runtime version did not change
examples.user_says: I submitted the upgrade but the spec_version is still 1
actions: Open runtime/src/lib.rs and confirm spec_version is still 1 rather than 2
Change spec_version to a value higher than the current on-chain version (e.g. 2)
Run cargo build --release to rebuild the Wasm binary
Re-upload the .compact.compressed.wasm via Developer > Extrinsics > sudo > system > setCode and resubmit
examples.result: Runtime version updates to 2 after resubmitting with the incremented spec_version.

skills.title: Spawn a Local Parachain Test Network with Zombienet
skills.description: Guides through spawning a local Polkadot parachain test network using Zombienet. You clone and build the Polkadot SDK parachain template, download polkadot/polkadot-prepare-worker/polkadot-execute-worker relay chain binaries and chain-spec-builder, download the Paseo runtime Wasm (requires INSERT_PASEO_RUNTIME_VERSION substitution), generate a local Paseo chain spec, write a network.toml, and run zombienet spawn. Use when you need a reproducible local multi-chain integration test environment. Trigger phrases: 'local parachain network', 'zombienet spawn', 'test parachain locally', 'relay chain and parachain local'. Requires Rust, Cargo, wasm32 target, Zombienet CLI, and Git. INSERT_PASEO_RUNTIME_VERSION in the Paseo runtime download URL must be replaced with an actual release tag before running.

runtime: Rust toolchain (stable) with wasm32-unknown-unknown target (rustup target add wasm32-unknown-unknown)
Zombienet CLI installed — see https://docs.polkadot.com/reference/tools/zombienet.md for installation instructions
network: GitHub release download access for Polkadot SDK binaries and Paseo runtime Wasm

steps.action: Clone and build the Polkadot SDK parachain template
steps.description: Clone the parachain template at branch stable2503 (substitute the branch matching your intended version). cargo build --release produces parachain-template-node at target/release/. The build takes 10-30 minutes. Note the full path to the clone directory — it is referenced in network.toml.

steps.action: Add the parachain binary to PATH
steps.description: Export target/release to PATH so parachain-template-node is accessible. This PATH change is session-scoped — re-run the export if you open a new terminal.

Download the three relay chain binaries from the Polkadot SDK releases. The release tag shown (polkadot-stable2503) matches the stable2503 parachain template branch — substitute the tag that matches your template branch. Check https://github.com/paritytech/polkadot-sdk/releases for available tags. The polkadot-prepare-worker and polkadot-execute-worker binaries must be in the same directory as polkadot.
steps.expected_output: bin/polkadot, bin/polkadot-prepare-worker, and bin/polkadot-execute-worker are present and executable

steps.description: Download the chain-spec-builder utility from the same Polkadot SDK release as the relay chain binaries.

STOP: Replace INSERT_PASEO_RUNTIME_VERSION with the actual Paseo runtime release tag before running this command. Find the latest release tag at https://github.com/paseo-network/runtimes/releases (e.g. v1.4.1). The command becomes: curl -L -o bin/paseo_runtime.compressed.wasm https://github.com/paseo-network/runtimes/releases/download/v1.4.1/paseo_runtime.compressed.wasm — substitute your chosen version. Running the literal INSERT_PASEO_RUNTIME_VERSION produces a 404 error.

commands: bin/chain-spec-builder -c configs/paseo-local.json create -r bin/paseo_runtime.compressed.wasm named-preset local_testnet
steps.description: Generate a local Paseo chain spec. The named-preset local_testnet includes Alice and Bob as pre-funded validators. Output: configs/paseo-local.json.

Create a file named network.toml in the project root with the following content. Replace ./polkadot-sdk-parachain-template with the actual path to your parachain template clone:

[settings]
timeout = 1000
provider = "native"

[relaychain]
chain_spec_path = "./configs/paseo-local.json"
default_command = "./bin/polkadot"

[[relaychain.nodes]]
name = "alice"
validator = true
rpc_port = 9944

[[relaychain.nodes]]
name = "bob"
validator = true
rpc_port = 9945

[[parachains]]
id = 1000

[[parachains.collators]]
name = "collator01"
command = "./polkadot-sdk-parachain-template/target/release/parachain-template-node"
rpc_port = 9988

Launch the network. Zombienet generates chain specs, starts Alice and Bob validators, registers and starts the parachain collator, and displays connection endpoints. Wait until RPC endpoints for all nodes are shown and the network is producing blocks. Press Ctrl+C to stop.
steps.expected_output: Network launched; RPC endpoints shown for alice (9944), bob (9945), and collator01 (9988)

steps.action: Verify block production on relay chain and parachain
steps.description: Query the block header from the relay chain (port 9944) and the parachain collator (port 9988). Both responses should contain a number field with an incrementing block number, confirming both are producing blocks.
steps.expected_output: Both responses contain a non-null number field showing the current block number

error_patterns.pattern: curl download returns 404 for Paseo runtime
error_patterns.cause: INSERT_PASEO_RUNTIME_VERSION was not replaced with an actual version tag.
error_patterns.resolution: Visit https://github.com/paseo-network/runtimes/releases for the latest release tag. Replace INSERT_PASEO_RUNTIME_VERSION with the correct tag (e.g. v1.4.1) in the step 5 curl command and re-run.

error_patterns.pattern: zombienet spawn fails with 'binary not found' for polkadot or parachain-template-node
error_patterns.cause: The polkadot binary path in network.toml is wrong, or target/release is not in PATH for the parachain template.
error_patterns.resolution: Verify ./bin/polkadot exists and is executable (ls -la bin/polkadot). For the collator, confirm the command path in network.toml points to the correct parachain-template-node binary. Re-run export PATH=$PATH:<parachain-template>/target/release if needed.

error_patterns.pattern: chain_getHeader returns null or parachain is not producing blocks
error_patterns.cause: The parachain collator has not yet registered or is waiting for relay chain finality.
error_patterns.resolution: Wait 30-60 seconds for the relay chain to finalize enough blocks for the parachain to start. Monitor the Zombienet terminal for collator registration messages.

pages.title: Zombienet
pages.relevance: Zombienet installation methods (executable, Nix, Docker), available providers, and full network.toml configuration reference including all relay chain, parachain, and collator options.

pages.title: Set Up the Polkadot SDK Parachain Template
pages.relevance: Setting up and building the Polkadot SDK parachain template used as the collator in this skill.

examples.scenario: Common scenario: spawn a local two-validator relay chain and one collator parachain
examples.user_says: Spawn a local parachain test network using Zombienet
actions: Clone polkadot-sdk-parachain-template at stable2503 and run cargo build --release
Export target/release to PATH
Download polkadot, polkadot-prepare-worker, polkadot-execute-worker from the polkadot-stable2503 release
Download chain-spec-builder from the same release
Replace INSERT_PASEO_RUNTIME_VERSION with the actual Paseo tag (e.g. v1.4.1) and download paseo_runtime.compressed.wasm
Generate Paseo local chain spec with chain-spec-builder and the local_testnet preset
Create network.toml with relay chain (Alice, Bob) and parachain collator config
Run zombienet spawn network.toml --provider native
Verify block production via curl JSON-RPC on ports 9944 and 9988
examples.result: Local network running with two relay chain validators and one parachain collator both producing blocks.

examples.scenario: Edge case: INSERT_PASEO_RUNTIME_VERSION not replaced, curl returns 404
examples.user_says: The Paseo runtime download fails with a 404 error
actions: Visit https://github.com/paseo-network/runtimes/releases to find the latest release tag
Replace INSERT_PASEO_RUNTIME_VERSION in the step 5 curl command with the correct tag
Re-run the curl command to download paseo_runtime.compressed.wasm
examples.result: Paseo runtime downloaded successfully; proceed with chain-spec-builder in step 6.

skills.title: Retrieve Polkadot Runtime Metadata
skills.description: Provides three methods for fetching Polkadot SDK runtime metadata: curl JSON-RPC (state_getMetadata, returns raw hex bytes), subxt CLI (human-readable JSON, ideal for inspection), and Polkadot.js Apps RPC UI (interactive). Use when building client libraries, inspecting pallet structure, or verifying runtime composition after an upgrade. Trigger phrases: 'get runtime metadata', 'state_getMetadata', 'inspect pallet calls', 'fetch chain metadata', 'subxt metadata', 'what pallets are on chain'. No private key or wallet required. The metadata output describes all pallets, storage items, calls, events, errors, and constants in the current runtime.

runtime: curl (Method A — available by default on macOS and most Linux distros)
subxt CLI (Method B — install with: cargo install subxt-cli)
network: An accessible RPC endpoint for the target Polkadot SDK node (e.g. https://rpc.polkadot.io for mainnet, or http://127.0.0.1:9944 for a local dev node)

steps.action: (Method A) Fetch metadata via curl JSON-RPC
steps.description: Call the state_getMetadata RPC method using curl. Replace https://rpc.polkadot.io with your target node's HTTP RPC endpoint. For a local node use http://127.0.0.1:9944. The response is a hex-encoded SCALE-encoded byte string — not human-readable. Use Method B or C if you need human-readable JSON.

steps.action: (Method B) Fetch metadata as human-readable JSON using subxt CLI
steps.description: Install the subxt CLI if not already installed: cargo install subxt-cli. Run subxt metadata to fetch and decode the metadata to JSON. Replace wss://rpc.polkadot.io with your node's WebSocket endpoint (wss:// for remote, ws:// for local). For a local dev node: subxt metadata --url ws://127.0.0.1:9944 --format json > spec.json. You can also explore metadata interactively at https://paritytech.github.io/subxt-explorer/.
steps.expected_output: spec.json created containing the runtime metadata in human-readable JSON format

steps.action: (Method C) Retrieve metadata via Polkadot.js Apps RPC UI
steps.description: Navigate to https://polkadot.js.org/apps/#/rpc and connect to your target node. Click the Developer dropdown and select RPC Calls. 1. Select state as the endpoint. 2. Select getMetadata(at) as the method. 3. Click Submit RPC Call. The metadata is returned in JSON format in the UI. This is the most interactive option but is not scriptable.

The metadata JSON contains three top-level sections: types (SCALE type registry indexed by u32 identifiers), pallets (array of pallet descriptors with storage, calls, events, errors, and constants for each pallet), and extrinsic (transaction version and signed extensions list). To find calls for a specific pallet: locate the pallet by name in the pallets array, follow the calls type index integer to the types section, then read the variant definitions. Example: find pallets[name='Sudo'].calls.type → find types[id=that u32].def.variant.variants for the available sudo calls. Note: type identifiers change between runtime versions — do not hardcode them.

error_patterns.pattern: curl returns connection refused or timeout
error_patterns.cause: The RPC endpoint URL is incorrect, the node is not running, or the endpoint does not accept HTTP connections.
error_patterns.resolution: Verify the RPC endpoint URL. For local nodes confirm the node is running and listening on port 9944. Some nodes only accept WebSocket connections — use subxt (Method B) with ws:// or wss:// instead of curl.

error_patterns.pattern: subxt metadata fails with 'failed to fetch metadata'
error_patterns.cause: The WebSocket URL is incorrect, the node is not running, or the subxt-cli version is incompatible with the node's metadata version.
error_patterns.resolution: Verify the WebSocket URL (wss:// for TLS, ws:// for local). Confirm the node is running. Update subxt-cli: cargo install subxt-cli --force.

error_patterns.pattern: Metadata output shows unexpected pallets or an empty pallets array
error_patterns.cause: The node connected to is a different chain than expected, or the genesis has not been customized.
error_patterns.resolution: Verify the RPC endpoint points to the intended chain. For local dev nodes ensure the chain spec matches the expected runtime.

supplementary_context.description: Load these pages for context on how metadata is used in client applications or SDK-level interactions.

pages.title: Subxt Rust API
pages.relevance: Subxt library usage for generating typed client code from runtime metadata — the primary consumer of metadata in Rust applications.

pages.title: Data Encoding
pages.relevance: SCALE encoding details that explain why the raw curl metadata output is hex-encoded bytes rather than readable JSON.

examples.scenario: Common scenario: inspect pallet list and call signatures of Polkadot mainnet
examples.user_says: Get the runtime metadata from the Polkadot mainnet node
actions: Run: subxt metadata --url wss://rpc.polkadot.io --format json > spec.json
Open spec.json and locate the pallets array
Find a specific pallet by name (e.g. Balances) and follow its calls type index to read available calls
examples.result: spec.json contains the complete runtime metadata in human-readable JSON; pallets, calls, storage items, events, and types are all inspectable.

examples.scenario: Edge case: inspect metadata from a local dev node
examples.user_says: How do I get the metadata from my local dev node at ws://127.0.0.1:9944?
actions: Verify the local node is running (polkadot-omni-node --dev or similar)
Run: subxt metadata --url ws://127.0.0.1:9944 --format json > local-spec.json
Or use curl: curl -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{"id":1,"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"state_getMetadata"}' http://127.0.0.1:9944
examples.result: Metadata from the local dev node retrieved; reflects the exact runtime version currently running locally.

skills.title: Query and Transact on Polkadot Chains with the Polkadot.js API
skills.description: Installs @polkadot/api and demonstrates creating a WsProvider connection, reading chain constants and on-chain storage, and submitting a signed balance transfer. Use when maintaining or migrating legacy TypeScript/JavaScript projects that already depend on @polkadot/api. Warning: this library is in maintenance mode — new projects should use Polkadot API (PAPI) or Dedot. Trigger phrases: 'polkadot.js api', '@polkadot/api', 'polkadot javascript sdk', 'use polkadot js'. Outcome: TypeScript script that connects to a node, reads account state, and sends a signed transaction.

network: WebSocket RPC endpoint for the target chain (e.g., wss://rpc.ibp.network/polkadot for Polkadot mainnet)

env_vars.name: WS_ENDPOINT
env_vars.description: WebSocket URL for the target chain (e.g., wss://services.polkadothub-rpc.com/testnet for Polkadot Hub TestNet).

env_vars.name: MNEMONIC
env_vars.description: 12 or 24-word seed phrase for the signing account. Required only for the transaction-sending step.

steps.action: Initialize ESM Node.js project and install @polkadot/api
commands: npm init -y && npm pkg set type=module
steps.description: Create a new directory named polkadot-api-demo and initialize it as an ESM Node.js project. The type=module flag is required because @polkadot packages use ESM-only imports. Install dotenv for secure credential loading.

Create .env with empty placeholders and exclude it from git. Stop and ask the user to edit the .env file directly — do NOT ask for the mnemonic or endpoint in chat. Set WS_ENDPOINT to the WebSocket URL for the target chain (e.g., wss://services.polkadothub-rpc.com/testnet for Polkadot Hub TestNet, or wss://rpc.ibp.network/polkadot for mainnet). Set MNEMONIC if you plan to send transactions. Wait for confirmation before proceeding.

steps.action: Create and run the transaction script

error_patterns.pattern: Error: WebSocket is not open / ECONNREFUSED
error_patterns.cause: The WS_ENDPOINT URL is unreachable or the protocol is wrong.
error_patterns.resolution: Verify WS_ENDPOINT in .env starts with wss:// (not https://). Check the node is online. For Polkadot Hub TestNet use wss://services.polkadothub-rpc.com/testnet.

error_patterns.pattern: SyntaxError: Cannot use import statement in a module
error_patterns.cause: The project is not configured as an ESM module.
error_patterns.resolution: Run 'npm pkg set type=module' in the polkadot-api-demo directory, then retry.

error_patterns.pattern: Error: Cannot find module '@polkadot/api'
error_patterns.cause: Dependencies not installed.
error_patterns.resolution: Run 'npm install @polkadot/api @polkadot/keyring dotenv' in the polkadot-api-demo directory.

error_patterns.pattern: RpcError: 1010: Invalid Transaction: Inability to pay some fees
error_patterns.cause: The sender account has insufficient funds to pay transaction fees.
error_patterns.resolution: Fund the sender account with tokens. For Polkadot Hub TestNet use the faucet at https://faucet.polkadot.io/.

skills.title: Bootstrap Parachain Development with Pop CLI
skills.description: Installs Pop CLI and uses it to set up Polkadot SDK build dependencies, scaffold a new parachain project, compile it in release mode, and spin up a local test network. Use when starting a new parachain from scratch and wanting an opinionated, guided scaffold over manual Polkadot SDK setup. Requires Rust stable toolchain and build dependencies from parachains/install-polkadot-sdk. Trigger phrases: 'pop cli', 'pop new parachain', 'bootstrap parachain pop', 'scaffold parachain with pop cli'. Outcome: local parachain network running with a freshly scaffolded template.

runtime: Rust stable toolchain (install via rustup: https://rustup.rs)
Polkadot SDK system dependencies — run the install script from https://docs.polkadot.com/parachains/install-polkadot-sdk.md first

steps.description: Install Pop CLI from crates.io. This requires the Rust stable toolchain and Polkadot SDK build dependencies (clang, cmake, protobuf-compiler, etc.). If cargo is not found, install Rust first: curl --proto '=https' --tlsv1.2 -sSf https://sh.rustup.rs | sh. Verify the install succeeded by running: pop --help

steps.action: Install Polkadot SDK build dependencies via Pop
steps.description: Run 'pop install' to let Pop CLI detect and install missing system dependencies required to build the Polkadot SDK (libssl, protobuf-compiler, cmake, etc.). This is equivalent to following the parachains/install-polkadot-sdk guide. The command may prompt for sudo password on Linux. Confirm each installation prompt.

Scaffold a new parachain named my-parachain using the default Polkadot SDK parachain template. Replace my-parachain with your desired project name (use kebab-case). Pop CLI creates the project directory with a complete Polkadot SDK parachain skeleton including runtime, node, and pallets.
steps.expected_output: my-parachain/ directory created with Polkadot SDK parachain template.

steps.action: Build the parachain in release mode
steps.description: Compile the parachain runtime and node binary. The first build takes 20-40 minutes. Subsequent builds are faster due to cargo caching. Replace my-parachain in the working_directory with the actual project directory name used in step 3.

Start a local Zombienet-based parachain test network using Pop CLI. Pop CLI looks for a network.toml configuration file in the project root. If no network.toml exists, create one by copying the example from the Pop CLI documentation or the project template. The network starts relay chain validators plus a parachain collator. Press Ctrl+C to stop.
steps.expected_output: Network launched. Relay chain and parachain collator endpoints printed to console.

error_patterns.resolution: Run 'pop install' to install missing system dependencies, then retry.

error_patterns.cause: No network configuration file found in the project root.
error_patterns.resolution: Create a network.toml file in the my-parachain directory. Refer to the Pop CLI documentation or the spawn-local-parachain-network-zombienet skill for a minimal example Zombienet TOML config.

skills.title: Deploy a Basic Smart Contract with Remix IDE
skills.description: Guides the user through deploying Remix IDE's default Storage.sol contract to Polkadot Hub TestNet via the browser-based Remix IDE and MetaMask wallet. All steps are browser GUI interactions — no local toolchain required. Use when testing the Polkadot Hub EVM environment, learning contract deployment basics, or demonstrating how Remix works with Polkadot Hub. Prerequisites: MetaMask installed and configured for Polkadot Hub TestNet (chain ID 420420417), testnet PAS tokens from https://faucet.polkadot.io/. Trigger phrases: 'deploy contract remix polkadot', 'remix ide polkadot hub', 'deploy storage contract remix'. Outcome: Storage contract deployed on Polkadot Hub TestNet.

runtime: Modern web browser (Chrome or Firefox recommended)
network: Polkadot Hub TestNet RPC: https://services.polkadothub-rpc.com/testnet
Chain ID: 420420417
tokens: Testnet PAS tokens from https://faucet.polkadot.io/ (rate limit: check faucet page)
wallet: MetaMask browser extension installed and configured for Polkadot Hub TestNet
Wallet funded with testnet PAS tokens

steps.action: Verify MetaMask is connected to Polkadot Hub TestNet
steps.description: Instruct the user: Open MetaMask and confirm the selected network is 'Polkadot Hub TestNet' (chain ID 420420417). If not configured, use the connect-wallet-polkadot-hub skill to add the network. Also confirm the account balance shows testnet PAS tokens. If the balance is zero, visit https://faucet.polkadot.io/ to request tokens. Wait for the user to confirm both conditions before proceeding.

steps.action: Open Remix IDE and locate Storage.sol
steps.description: Instruct the user: Open https://remix.ethereum.org in the browser. In the left file explorer panel, expand the 'contracts/' folder. Click on '1_Storage.sol' to open it in the editor. This is the default Remix sample contract — no changes needed.

Instruct the user: Click the Solidity Compiler icon (second icon in the left sidebar). Confirm the compiler version is 0.8.x or higher. Click the blue 'Compile 1_Storage.sol' button. Wait for the green checkmark indicating successful compilation. If there are errors, verify the compiler version matches the pragma in the file.
steps.expected_output: Green checkmark on the Solidity Compiler sidebar icon; no errors shown.

steps.action: Deploy to Polkadot Hub TestNet via MetaMask
steps.description: Instruct the user: Click the Deploy and Run Transactions icon (fourth icon in the left sidebar). In the Environment dropdown, select 'Injected Provider - MetaMask'. MetaMask will prompt for permission — click Connect. Confirm the Account field shows your MetaMask address and the Network shows Polkadot Hub TestNet (chain ID 420420417). Click the orange 'Deploy' button. MetaMask will open a transaction confirmation — click Confirm. Wait for the transaction to be mined (typically 15-30 seconds on TestNet).
steps.expected_output: Deployed contract appears in the 'Deployed Contracts' section at the bottom of the Deploy panel.

steps.action: Interact with the deployed Storage contract
steps.description: Instruct the user: In the 'Deployed Contracts' panel, expand the deployed Storage contract. To write a value: enter a number in the field next to the 'store' button and click 'store'. Confirm the MetaMask transaction. To read the stored value: click the 'retrieve' button (no transaction needed). The return value shows in the Remix console below.
steps.expected_output: retrieve() returns the number stored in the previous store() call.

skills.title: Deploy and Interact with Smart Contracts on Polkadot Hub Using Web3.py
skills.description: Python workflow for compiling Solidity contracts with py-solc-x, deploying them to Polkadot Hub TestNet (chain ID 420420417) using Web3.py, and reading/writing contract state. Uses a Python virtualenv, .env file for the private key, and three scripts: compile.py, deploy.py, interact.py. Use when your project is Python-based and you need EVM contract interaction without Node.js. Key capabilities: virtualenv setup, py-solc-x Solidity compilation, Web3.py contract deployment, transaction signing with a local account, contract function calls. NOTE: Web3.js (the JavaScript library) has been sunset — for new JavaScript/TypeScript projects prefer Ethers.js or viem. This skill covers Web3.py (Python), which is actively maintained. Trigger phrases: 'web3.py polkadot', 'python smart contract deployment', 'deploy contract python', 'web3 python tutorial'.

network: Polkadot Hub TestNet — RPC https://services.polkadothub-rpc.com/testnet, chain ID 420420417
tokens: Small PAS balance for deployment gas — obtain from https://faucet.polkadot.io/
wallet: An EVM private key (PRIVATE_KEY stored in .env — never enter in chat)

env_vars.name: PRIVATE_KEY
env_vars.description: EVM account private key (with or without 0x prefix) for signing deployment and write transactions. Do NOT ask the user to type or paste their private key in the chat — they should edit the .env file directly.

steps.action: Create project directory and Python virtual environment
steps.description: Create a new directory named 'web3py-contracts', create a Python virtual environment inside it, and activate it. On Windows use 'venv\Scripts\activate' instead.

Install web3 (Web3.py — Ethereum/EVM Python client), py-solc-x (Python wrapper for the Solidity compiler), and python-dotenv (loads .env variables). This may take a minute as py-solc-x downloads a Solidity compiler binary.

Create a file named '.env' in the web3py-contracts directory. Do NOT ask the user to type or paste their private key in the chat — instruct them to open the .env file in a text editor and add:

PRIVATE_KEY=0xYOUR_PRIVATE_KEY_HERE

Replace 0xYOUR_PRIVATE_KEY_HERE with the actual private key for the funded TestNet account. Also create a .gitignore file and add '.env' to it to prevent accidental commits:

echo '.env' > .gitignore

steps.action: Create compile.py to compile the Solidity contract
Create deploy.py to deploy the contract
steps.description:
Create a file named 'deploy.py' with the following content:

```python
import json
import os
from web3 import Web3
from dotenv import load_dotenv

load_dotenv()

RPC_URL = 'https://services.polkadothub-rpc.com/testnet'
CHAIN_ID = 420420417
PRIVATE_KEY = os.environ['PRIVATE_KEY']

w3 = Web3(Web3.HTTPProvider(RPC_URL))
assert w3.is_connected(), 'Failed to connect to RPC endpoint'

account = w3.eth.account.from_key(PRIVATE_KEY)
print(f'Deploying from account: {account.address}')

with open('compiled.json') as f:
    compiled = json.load(f)

contract_data = compiled['contracts']['Storage.sol']['Storage']
abi = contract_data['abi']
bytecode = contract_data['evm']['bytecode']['object']

Contract = w3.eth.contract(abi=abi, bytecode=bytecode)
nonce = w3.eth.get_transaction_count(account.address)

tx = Contract.constructor().build_transaction({
    'chainId': CHAIN_ID,
    'from': account.address,
    'nonce': nonce,
    'gasPrice': w3.to_wei('1000', 'gwei'),
})
tx['gas'] = w3.eth.estimate_gas(tx)

signed = account.sign_transaction(tx)
tx_hash = w3.eth.send_raw_transaction(signed.raw_transaction)
receipt = w3.eth.wait_for_transaction_receipt(tx_hash)

print(f'Contract deployed at: {receipt.contractAddress}')
with open('contract_address.txt', 'w') as f:
    f.write(receipt.contractAddress)
print('Address saved to contract_address.txt')
```

The RPC URL is set to https://services.polkadothub-rpc.com/testnet and chain ID to 420420417 — no substitutions needed.
steps.expected_output:
Deploying from account: 0x...
Contract deployed at: 0x...
Address saved to contract_address.txt

steps.action: Create interact.py to call store and retrieve
steps.description:
Create a file named 'interact.py' with the following content:

```python
import json
import os
from web3 import Web3
from dotenv import load_dotenv

load_dotenv()

RPC_URL = 'https://services.polkadothub-rpc.com/testnet'
CHAIN_ID = 420420417
PRIVATE_KEY = os.environ['PRIVATE_KEY']

w3 = Web3(Web3.HTTPProvider(RPC_URL))
account = w3.eth.account.from_key(PRIVATE_KEY)

with open('contract_address.txt') as f:
    contract_address = f.read().strip()

with open('compiled.json') as f:
    compiled = json.load(f)
abi = compiled['contracts']['Storage.sol']['Storage']['abi']

contract = w3.eth.contract(address=contract_address, abi=abi)

# Read current value
value = contract.functions.retrieve().call()
print(f'Current stored value: {value}')

# Write new value
nonce = w3.eth.get_transaction_count(account.address)
tx = contract.functions.store(42).build_transaction({
    'chainId': CHAIN_ID,
    'from': account.address,
    'nonce': nonce,
    'gasPrice': w3.to_wei('1000', 'gwei'),
})
tx['gas'] = w3.eth.estimate_gas(tx)
signed = account.sign_transaction(tx)
tx_hash = w3.eth.send_raw_transaction(signed.raw_transaction)
receipt = w3.eth.wait_for_transaction_receipt(tx_hash)
print(f'store(42) tx: {receipt.transactionHash.hex()}')

# Verify
value = contract.functions.retrieve().call()
print(f'Updated stored value: {value}')
```
steps.expected_output:
Current stored value: 0
store(42) tx: 0x...
Updated stored value: 42

skills.title: Interact with the ERC-20 Precompile on Polkadot Hub via Remix
skills.description: Guides through using the ERC-20 precompile on Polkadot Hub, which wraps the native PAS token as an ERC-20 compatible interface at a fixed precompile address. Workflow: connect MetaMask to Polkadot Hub TestNet, load the precompile ABI in Remix IDE, and call read functions (balanceOf, allowance) and write functions (transfer, approve, transferFrom). Use when you need to interact with native PAS as an ERC-20 token from a Solidity contract or Remix. Key capabilities: MetaMask wallet connection, Remix At Address deployment against precompile, ERC-20 standard function calls on the native token. NOTE: The exact precompile address is documented in the source page — load https://docs.polkadot.com/smart-contracts/precompiles/erc20.md to find it. Trigger phrases: 'erc20 precompile polkadot', 'native token as erc20', 'pas erc20 interface', 'precompile erc20 remix'.

skills.title: Interact with the Storage Precompile on Polkadot Hub via Remix
skills.description: Six-step workflow for using the Storage precompile on Polkadot Hub via Remix IDE. The Storage precompile provides a persistent key-value store accessible from smart contracts, mapping bytes32 keys to bytes values. Workflow: connect MetaMask, find the precompile address from the source page, create the precompile interface in Remix, load it via At Address, and call setBytes/getBytes. Use when you need on-chain persistent storage accessible directly from precompile calls without deploying a storage contract. Key capabilities: bytes32 key encoding, bytes value storage, Storage precompile ABI definition in Remix, MetaMask transaction signing. NOTE: The exact precompile address is documented in the source page — load https://docs.polkadot.com/smart-contracts/precompiles/storage.md to find it. Trigger phrases: 'storage precompile polkadot', 'setBytes getBytes precompile', 'polkadot hub key value storage', 'precompile storage remix'.

steps.action: Call setBytes to store a value
steps.description: Delegate to the user: In the Deployed Contracts section, expand the IStorage instance. Expand the 'setBytes' function. In the 'key' field, enter a bytes32 value — for example, a 32-byte hex string: 0x6d79546573744b657900000000000000000000000000000000000000000000000 (this is 'myTestKey' zero-padded to 32 bytes). In the 'value' field, enter the hex-encoded bytes value you want to store — for example: 0x68656c6c6f (which is 'hello' in UTF-8). Click 'transact' (orange button). MetaMask will prompt for confirmation — approve it. Wait for the transaction to confirm.

steps.action: Call getBytes to retrieve the stored value
steps.description: Delegate to the user: In the IStorage instance, expand 'getBytes'. Enter the same bytes32 key used in setBytes. Click 'call' (blue button). The output displays the stored bytes value in hex. To decode: the hex 0x68656c6c6f decodes to 'hello' in UTF-8.

error_patterns.pattern: Remix: At Address button grayed out

error_patterns.resolution: Compile IStorage.sol first via the Solidity Compiler tab, then return to Deploy and Run. Ensure 'IStorage' is selected in the Contract dropdown before clicking At Address.

error_patterns.pattern: getBytes returns 0x (empty bytes)
error_patterns.cause: The key used in getBytes does not match the key used in setBytes, or the setBytes transaction did not confirm.
error_patterns.resolution: Ensure the bytes32 key in getBytes is identical to the one used in setBytes. Check Remix console for the setBytes transaction hash to confirm it was mined.

error_patterns.pattern: MetaMask: Transaction failed with out of gas
error_patterns.cause: Gas estimate too low for setBytes with a large value.
error_patterns.resolution: In MetaMask, increase the gas limit manually before confirming the setBytes transaction.

skills.title: Interact with the System Precompile on Polkadot Hub via Remix
skills.description: Guides through using the System precompile on Polkadot Hub via Remix IDE. The System precompile exposes Substrate-native operations to EVM contracts: BLAKE2b/BLAKE2s hashing, sr25519 signature verification, account existence checks, and contract lifecycle operations (self-destruct, code retrieval). Workflow: connect MetaMask, find the precompile address from the source page, create the interface in Remix, load it via At Address, and call individual functions. NOTE: Several example inputs (sig, pubKey) are placeholder values — the step descriptions mark these as INSERT_SIGNATURE and INSERT_PUBLIC_KEY with instructions on how to obtain them. NOTE: The exact precompile address is documented in the source page — load https://docs.polkadot.com/smart-contracts/precompiles/system.md to find it. Use when you need Substrate cryptographic primitives from an EVM contract. Trigger phrases: 'system precompile polkadot', 'blake2 hash solidity', 'sr25519 verification evm', 'polkadot hub system precompile remix'.

runtime: Modern web browser (Chrome or Firefox recommended)
wallet: MetaMask installed with Polkadot Hub TestNet configured (chain ID 420420417)
network: Polkadot Hub TestNet — chain ID 420420417, RPC https://services.polkadothub-rpc.com/testnet
tokens: Small PAS balance for write transactions — obtain from https://faucet.polkadot.io/

steps.action: Find the System precompile address from the source page
steps.description: Delegate to the user: Load https://docs.polkadot.com/smart-contracts/precompiles/system.md to find the exact System precompile address for Polkadot Hub TestNet. Note this address — it will be used in step 4 as the 'At Address' target in Remix.

steps.description: Delegate to the user: Open MetaMask and switch to Polkadot Hub TestNet (chain ID 420420417). If the network is not added, use the add-polkadot-hub-testnet-metamask skill. Confirm a non-zero PAS balance.

steps.action: Create the System precompile interface in Remix
Connect Remix to MetaMask and load the precompile using At Address
steps.description: Delegate to the user: Click the Deploy and Run Transactions tab in Remix. Set Environment to 'Injected Provider - MetaMask' and confirm the MetaMask connection. Verify the network shows 'Custom (420420417) network'. Select 'ISystem' in the Contract dropdown. Paste the System precompile address (from step 1) into 'At Address'. Click 'At Address'. An ISystem instance should appear in the Deployed Contracts section.

Delegate to the user: In the Deployed Contracts section, expand the ISystem instance. Expand the 'blake2b' function. In the 'data' field, enter hex-encoded bytes to hash — for example: 0x68656c6c6f (UTF-8 for 'hello'). Click 'call' (blue). The output is the BLAKE2b hash of the input as hex-encoded bytes. You can also call 'blake2s' with the same input to get the BLAKE2s variant.
steps.expected_output: bytes: hex-encoded BLAKE2b hash of the input data

steps.description:
Delegate to the user: Expand the 'verifySignature' function. To use this function you need three values:

- INSERT_SIGNATURE: Replace with a real sr25519 signature as hex bytes (64 bytes, 128 hex chars). Generate a signature using a Polkadot.js signer or subkey tool: subkey sign --scheme sr25519 --suri '//Alice' --message 'your_message'

- INSERT_PUBLIC_KEY: Replace with the sr25519 public key (32 bytes, 64 hex chars) corresponding to the signer.

- message: The message bytes that were signed (hex-encoded).

Enter the three values in the 'sig', 'pubKey', and 'message' fields respectively, then click 'call'. The function returns true if the signature is valid, false otherwise.
steps.expected_output: bool: true if the sr25519 signature is valid for the given pubKey and message

steps.action: Call accountExists to check account presence
steps.description: Delegate to the user: Expand the 'accountExists' function. Enter an Ethereum-format address (0x...) in the 'account' field. Click 'call'. The function returns true if the account exists on-chain (i.e. has been funded or deployed), false if it has never received any funds or activity.
steps.expected_output: bool: true if the account exists on Polkadot Hub TestNet, false otherwise

error_patterns.pattern: Remix: call reverts on verifySignature with invalid inputs
error_patterns.cause: The sig or pubKey bytes are not the correct length or format for sr25519.
error_patterns.resolution: sr25519 signatures are 64 bytes (128 hex chars with 0x prefix = 130 chars total). Public keys are 32 bytes (64 hex chars). Use the subkey tool to generate a valid signature: subkey sign --scheme sr25519 --suri '//Alice' --message 'test'. Ensure all inputs are hex-encoded with 0x prefix.

error_patterns.pattern: At Address: no contract at address
error_patterns.cause: Incorrect precompile address entered, or MetaMask connected to the wrong network.
error_patterns.resolution: Re-load the source page to get the correct System precompile address. Verify MetaMask shows chain ID 420420417.

error_patterns.pattern: ISystem.sol compilation error: function not found in interface
error_patterns.cause: The interface in ISystem.sol may not match the exact ABI exposed by the precompile.
error_patterns.resolution: Load https://docs.polkadot.com/smart-contracts/precompiles/system.md for the authoritative ABI and update the interface accordingly.

skills.title: Interact with the XCM Precompile via Remix IDE
skills.description: Guides you through creating an IXcm Solidity interface in Remix, loading the XCM precompile, and calling weighMessage, execute, and send functions. Use when you need to test XCM message dispatch from a smart contract, estimate XCM execution weight, or send cross-chain messages from Polkadot Hub via Solidity. All interaction is browser-based using Remix IDE with MetaMask. Trigger phrases: 'XCM precompile', 'send XCM from contract', 'weighMessage', 'xcm execute', 'cross-chain from Solidity', 'IXcm interface'. Output: XCM weight estimates and on-chain transaction receipts.

runtime: Modern web browser (Chrome or Firefox recommended)
wallet: MetaMask installed with Polkadot Hub TestNet configured (chain ID 420420417)
network: Polkadot Hub TestNet — chain ID 420420417, RPC https://services.polkadothub-rpc.com/testnet
tokens: Small PAS balance for write transactions — obtain from https://faucet.polkadot.io/

steps.action: Find the XCM precompile address and IXcm ABI from the source page
steps.description: Delegate to the user: Load https://docs.polkadot.com/smart-contracts/precompiles/xcm.md to find the exact XCM precompile address for Polkadot Hub TestNet and the IXcm interface definition. Note the address — it will be used in step 4 as the 'At Address' target in Remix. The source page is the authoritative reference for the ABI.

steps.action: Connect MetaMask to Polkadot Hub TestNet
steps.description: Delegate to the user: Open MetaMask and switch to Polkadot Hub TestNet (chain ID 420420417). If the network is not added, use the connect-wallet-polkadot-hub skill. Confirm a non-zero PAS balance for gas fees.

steps.action: Create the IXcm interface in Remix and compile it
steps.description: Delegate to the user: Open https://remix.ethereum.org. Create a new Solidity file named 'IXcm.sol'. Copy the IXcm interface definition from the source page (step 1) — the interface includes at minimum: weighMessage(bytes dest, bytes message), execute(bytes message, uint64 maxWeight), and send(bytes dest, bytes message). The source page is authoritative; use its exact ABI. Go to the Solidity Compiler tab, select version 0.8.20, and click 'Compile IXcm.sol'. Confirm there are no compilation errors.

steps.action: Load the XCM precompile using At Address in Remix
steps.description: Delegate to the user: Click the Deploy and Run Transactions tab in Remix. Set Environment to 'Injected Provider - MetaMask'. Confirm the MetaMask connection — verify the network shows 'Custom (420420417) network'. Select 'IXcm' in the Contract dropdown. Paste the XCM precompile address (from step 1) into the 'At Address' field. Click 'At Address'. An IXcm instance should appear in the Deployed Contracts section.

steps.action: Call weighMessage to estimate XCM execution cost
steps.description: Delegate to the user: In the Deployed Contracts section, expand the IXcm instance. Expand the 'weighMessage' function. For 'dest': provide the SCALE-encoded multilocation bytes of the destination chain (see source page for encoding examples). For 'message': provide the SCALE-encoded XCM message bytes (the source page includes a complete encoded example). Click 'call' (blue button — read-only). The output is the estimated weight (refTime and proofSize) needed to execute the message.
steps.expected_output: Weight object with refTime and proofSize fields representing the estimated execution cost

steps.action: Call execute to run an XCM message locally
steps.description: Delegate to the user: Expand the 'execute' function. For 'message': provide SCALE-encoded XCM bytes (use the example from the source page). For 'maxWeight': enter the refTime value returned by weighMessage in step 5, or a conservative upper bound. Click 'transact' (orange button — state-changing). MetaMask will prompt for gas approval — confirm. The call executes the XCM message in the context of the calling address on the local chain.
steps.expected_output: Transaction receipt with status 1 (success); XCM execution event visible in Remix logs

steps.action: Call send to dispatch an XCM to another chain
steps.description: Delegate to the user: Expand the 'send' function. For 'dest': provide SCALE-encoded multilocation bytes for the target parachain (refer to source page for encoding). For 'message': provide SCALE-encoded XCM message bytes. Click 'transact' (orange). MetaMask prompts for gas — confirm. The XCM is dispatched toward the destination chain. Delivery and execution on the remote chain are asynchronous — use the xcm-tools skill to monitor delivery if needed.
steps.expected_output: Transaction receipt with status 1; XcmMessageSent event in Remix logs

error_patterns.pattern: Remix: transaction reverted on execute or send
error_patterns.cause: SCALE-encoded XCM bytes are malformed or the weight limit is too low.
error_patterns.resolution: Verify the encoding against the example on the source page. Use weighMessage first to obtain the correct maxWeight. Ensure the correct precompile address is used for TestNet.

error_patterns.pattern: At Address: no contract at address
error_patterns.cause: Incorrect precompile address entered, or MetaMask is connected to the wrong network.
error_patterns.resolution: Reload the source page to get the authoritative XCM precompile address. Verify MetaMask shows chain ID 420420417.

error_patterns.pattern: IXcm.sol compilation error: function not found
error_patterns.cause: The interface definition does not match the ABI the precompile exposes.
error_patterns.resolution: Load https://docs.polkadot.com/smart-contracts/precompiles/xcm.md for the authoritative ABI and update IXcm.sol accordingly.

error_patterns.pattern: MetaMask: insufficient funds for gas
error_patterns.cause: PAS balance too low to cover gas on Polkadot Hub TestNet (base fee: 1000 gwei).
error_patterns.resolution: Obtain PAS tokens from https://faucet.polkadot.io/. Ensure the account has at least 0.01 PAS before submitting state-changing calls.

Create and compile IXcm.sol in Remix

Call weighMessage with encoded destination and message bytes
Read the returned weight estimate
examples.result: Weight estimate (refTime and proofSize) indicating the execution cost on the destination chain
